**Pakistan**,[[f]](#cite_note-13) officially the **Islamic Republic of Pakistan**,[[g]](#cite_note-14) is a country in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). It is the [fifth-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"), with a population of over 241.5 million,[[d]](#cite_note-fn3-10) having the [second-largest Muslim population](/wiki/Islam_by_country#Countries "Islam by country") as of 2023. [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad") is the nation's capital, while [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") is [its largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Pakistan_by_population "List of cities in Pakistan by population") and [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre"). Pakistan is the [33rd-largest country by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"). Bounded by the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") on the south, the [Gulf of Oman](/wiki/Gulf_of_Oman "Gulf of Oman") on the southwest, and the [Sir Creek](/wiki/Sir_Creek "Sir Creek") on the southeast, it shares land borders with [India](/wiki/India "India") to the east; [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") to the west; [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") to the southwest; and [China](/wiki/China "China") to the northeast. It shares a maritime border with [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman") in the Gulf of Oman, and is separated from [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") in the northwest by Afghanistan's narrow [Wakhan Corridor](/wiki/Wakhan_Corridor "Wakhan Corridor").

Pakistan is the site of [several ancient cultures](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan "History of Pakistan"), including the 8,500-year-old [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") site of [Mehrgarh](/wiki/Mehrgarh "Mehrgarh") in [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan"), the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") of the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"),[[8]](#cite_note-Wright-2009-15) and the ancient [Gandhara civilisation](/wiki/Gandhara_civilisation "Gandhara civilisation").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBadian1987-16) The regions that compose the modern state of Pakistan were the realm of multiple empires and dynasties, including the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), the [Maurya](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire"), the [Kushan](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), the [Gupta](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire");[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynbrandt2009-17) the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") in its southern regions, the [Hindu Shahis](/wiki/Hindu_Shahis "Hindu Shahis"), the [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids"), the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), the [Samma](/wiki/Samma_dynasty "Samma dynasty"), the [Shah Miris](/wiki/Shah_Miris "Shah Miris"), the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"),[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler1969-18) and finally, the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") from 1858 to 1947.

Spurred by the [Pakistan Movement](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement"), which sought a homeland for the Muslims of [British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India"), and election victories in 1946 by the [All-India Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League"), Pakistan gained independence in 1947 after the [partition of British India](/wiki/Partition_of_British_India "Partition of British India"), which awarded separate statehood to its Muslim-majority regions and was accompanied by an unparalleled mass migration and loss of life.[[12]](#cite_note-Copland-2001-19)[[13]](#cite_note-Metcalf-2006-20) Initially [a dominion](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") of the [British Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), Pakistan [adopted a republican constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_(1956%E2%80%931958) "Constitution of Pakistan (1956–1958)") in 1956 and became an [Islamic republic](/wiki/Islamic_republic "Islamic republic") with two geographically separate provinces, [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan") and [West Pakistan](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan"). East Pakistan seceded as the new country of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") in 1971 after a [nine-month-long civil war](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"). In the following four decades, Pakistan has been ruled by governments that alternated between civilian and military, democratic and authoritarian, relatively [secular](/wiki/Secularism_in_Pakistan "Secularism in Pakistan") and Islamist.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETalbot2016-21)

Pakistan is considered a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") nation, with the world's [seventh-largest standing armed forces](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel"). It is a declared [nuclear-weapons state](/wiki/List_of_states_with_nuclear_weapons "List of states with nuclear weapons"), and is ranked amongst the emerging and growth-leading economies,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiaBurton2023-22) with a large and rapidly growing middle class.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERais2017-23)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECornwallEdwards2014-24) Pakistan's political history since independence has been characterized by periods of significant economic and military growth as well as those of political and economic instability. It is an [ethnically](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Pakistan "Ethnic groups in Pakistan") and [linguistically](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan") diverse country, with similarly diverse [geography](/wiki/Geography_of_Pakistan "Geography of Pakistan") and [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Pakistan "Wildlife of Pakistan"). The country continues to face [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Pakistan "Poverty in Pakistan"), [illiteracy](/wiki/Literate_Pakistan_Foundation "Literate Pakistan Foundation"), [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Pakistan "Corruption in Pakistan"), and [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism_in_Pakistan "Terrorism in Pakistan").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoseph2016-25)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaqir2018-26)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESATP2024-27) Pakistan is a member of the United Nations, the [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation"), the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation"), and the [Islamic Military Counter-Terrorism Coalition](/wiki/Islamic_Military_Counter_Terrorism_Coalition "Islamic Military Counter Terrorism Coalition"), and is designated as a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") by the United States.

## Etymology

The name *Pakistan* was coined by [Choudhry Rahmat Ali](/wiki/Choudhry_Rahmat_Ali "Choudhry Rahmat Ali"), a [Pakistan Movement](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement") activist, who in January 1933 first published it (originally as "Pakstan") in a pamphlet *[Now or Never](/wiki/Pakistan_Declaration "Pakistan Declaration")*, using it as an [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAziz1987-28)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaqibMalik2018-29)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahiri2023-30) Rahmat Ali explained: "It is composed of letters taken from the names of all our homelands, Indian and Asian, [**P**anjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)"), [**A**fghania](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%932010) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–2010)"), [**K**ashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)"), [**S**indh](/wiki/Sind_Division "Sind Division"), and [Baluchis**tan**](/wiki/Baluchistan_Agency "Baluchistan Agency")." He added, "Pakistan is both a [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") word... It means the land of the Paks, the spiritually pure and clean."[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETummala1996-31) Etymologists note that [پاک](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D9%BE%D8%A7%DA%A9#Persian "wikt:پاک") *pāk*, is 'pure' in Persian and [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") and the Persian suffix [ـستان](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D9%80%D8%B3%D8%AA%D8%A7%D9%86#Persian "wikt:ـستان") *[-stan](/wiki/-stan "-stan")* means 'land' or 'place of'.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaqibMalik2018-29)

Rahmat Ali's concept of Pakistan only related to the northwestern area of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). He also proposed the name "Banglastan" for the Muslim areas of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and "Osmanistan" for [Hyderabad State](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State"), as well as a political federation between the three.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnand1991-32)

## History

Main article: [History of Pakistan](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan "History of Pakistan")

See also: [Timeline of Pakistani history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Pakistani_history "Timeline of Pakistani history")

### Prehistory and antiquity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Mohenjo-daro_Priesterk%C3%B6nig.jpeg/250px-Mohenjo-daro_Priesterk%C3%B6nig.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Mohenjo-daro_Priesterk%C3%B6nig.jpeg)

*[Priest-King](/wiki/Priest-king_(sculpture) "Priest-king (sculpture)")* from [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro") (c. 2500 BCE)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2017-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Cremation_Urn_with_Lid_LACMA_AC1994.234.8a-b.jpg/250px-Cremation_Urn_with_Lid_LACMA_AC1994.234.8a-b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cremation_Urn_with_Lid_LACMA_AC1994.234.8a-b.jpg)

Cremation urn, [Gandhara grave culture](/wiki/Gandhara_grave_culture "Gandhara grave culture"), Swat Valley, c. 1200 BCE[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrison2017-34)

Some of the earliest ancient human civilisations in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") originated from areas encompassing present-day Pakistan.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllchinPetraglia2007-35) The earliest known stone tools in the region, dating to the [Lower Palaeolithic](/wiki/Lower_Palaeolithic "Lower Palaeolithic") (~2 million years ago), were discovered in the [Soan Valley](/wiki/Soan_Valley "Soan Valley") of [northern Pakistan](/wiki/Northern_Pakistan "Northern Pakistan").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERendellDennell1985-36) The [Indus region](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"), which covers most of the present-day Pakistan, was the site of several successive ancient cultures including the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") (7000–4300 [BCE](/wiki/BCE "BCE")) site of [Mehrgarh](/wiki/Mehrgarh "Mehrgarh"),[[30]](#cite_note-Coningham-Young-2015-37)[[31]](#cite_note-Fisher-2018-38)[[32]](#cite_note-Dyson-2018-39) and the 5,000-year history of urban life in South Asia to the various sites of the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation"), including [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro") and [Harappa](/wiki/Harappa "Harappa").[[33]](#cite_note-Allchin-1982-40)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalesKenoyerAlcock1986-41)

Following the decline of the Indus Valley civilisation, semi-nomadic [Indo-European Aryans](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples") migrated into the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") around 2000 BCE, perhaps by way of the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass"). They fused with the indigenous Harappan culture of the Indus Valley, and elements of the pre-Aryan spiritual traditions were assimilated into the developing Vedic tradition.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConsiglio2015-42) This cultural milieu shaped the [Gandhara civilization](/wiki/Gandhara_civilization "Gandhara civilization"), which flourished at the crossroads of India, Central Asia, and the Middle East, connecting [trade routes](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") and absorbing cultural influences from diverse civilisations.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBehrendt2007-43) By the early Vedic period, parts of the Indus region in present-day Pakistan were populated by numerous tribes that were beginning to coalesce into chieftain-led clans and early kingdoms.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood2022-44) During this period, the [Vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas"), the oldest [scriptures](/wiki/Scriptures "Scriptures") of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), were composed.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberlies2023-45)[[h]](#cite_note-Rigveda-46)

### Classical period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Gandhara_Buddha_%28tnm%29.jpeg/250px-Gandhara_Buddha_%28tnm%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Gandhara_Buddha_(tnm).jpeg)

*[Standing Buddha](/wiki/Standing_Buddha "Standing Buddha")* from [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") (1st–2nd century CE)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStonard2017-47)

The western regions of Pakistan [became part](/wiki/Achaemenid_conquest_of_the_Indus_Valley "Achaemenid conquest of the Indus Valley") of [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") around 517 BCE.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDandamaev2023-48) In 326 BCE, [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") conquered the region by defeating various local rulers, most notably, the King [Porus](/wiki/Porus "Porus"), at [Jhelum](/wiki/Battle_of_Hydaspes "Battle of Hydaspes").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadasivan2011-49) Among the major powers that ruled the region were the [Mauryas](/wiki/Mauryas "Mauryas") (322–185 BCE), during which [Ashoka the Great](/wiki/Ashoka_the_Great "Ashoka the Great") extended the empire.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2022114-50)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooke2017-51) The [Indo-Greek Kingdom](/wiki/Indo-Greek_Kingdom "Indo-Greek Kingdom") founded by [Demetrius of Bactria](/wiki/Demetrius_I_of_Bactria "Demetrius I of Bactria") (180–165 BCE) included [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") and reached its greatest extent under [Menander](/wiki/Menander_I "Menander I") (165–150 BCE), prospering the [Greco-Buddhist](/wiki/Greco-Buddhism "Greco-Buddhism") culture in the region.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollitt1986-52)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuintanilla2007-53)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKubica2023-54) [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") had one of the earliest universities and centres of higher education in the world, which was established during the late Vedic period in the 6th century BCE.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestmoreland2019-55) The ancient university was documented by the invading forces of Alexander the Great and was also recorded by Chinese pilgrims in the 4th or 5th century CE.[[48]](#cite_note-Needham-1994-56)[[49]](#cite_note-Kulke-Rothermund-2016-57)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMookerji1989-58) At its zenith, the [Rai dynasty](/wiki/Rai_dynasty "Rai dynasty") (489–632 CE) ruled [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") and the surrounding territories.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanerjee2022-59)

### Medieval period

The Arab conqueror [Muhammad ibn Qasim](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Qasim "Muhammad ibn Qasim") conquered Sindh and some regions of Punjab in 711 CE.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMufti2013-60) The Pakistan government's official chronology claims this as the time when the foundation of Pakistan was laid.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoodbhoy2023-61) The early medieval period (642–1219 CE) witnessed the spread of Islam in the region.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavendish2006318-62) Before the arrival of Islam beginning in the 8th century, the region of Pakistan was home to a diverse plethora of faiths, including [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") and [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism").[[55]](#cite_note-Stubbs-Thomson-2016-63)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalik200647-64) During this period, [Sufi](/wiki/Sufi "Sufi") [missionaries](/wiki/Dawah "Dawah") played a pivotal role in converting a majority of the regional population to Islam.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELapidus2014-65) Upon the defeat of the [Turk](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") and [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") dynasties which governed the [Kabul Valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley"), [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara"), and western Punjab in the 7th to 11th centuries CE, [several successive Muslim empires](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent") ruled over the region, including the [Ghaznavid Empire](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") (975–1187 CE), the [Ghorid](/wiki/Ghorid "Ghorid") Kingdom, and the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") (1206–1526 CE).[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamad2011-66) The [Lodi dynasty](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty"), the last of the Delhi Sultanate, was replaced by the Mughal Empire (1526–1857 CE).[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaroqhi2019-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/View_of_Makli_by_Usman_Ghani_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-View_of_Makli_by_Usman_Ghani_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Makli_by_Usman_Ghani_(cropped).jpg)

[Makli Necropolis](/wiki/Makli_Necropolis "Makli Necropolis"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") rose to prominence as a major funerary site during the [Samma dynasty](/wiki/Samma_dynasty "Samma dynasty").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJunejo2020-68)

The Mughals introduced Persian literature and high culture, establishing the roots of [Indo-Persian culture](/wiki/Indo-Persian_culture "Indo-Persian culture") in the region.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECanfield2002-69) In the region of modern-day Pakistan, key cities during the Mughal period were [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") and [Thatta](/wiki/Thatta "Thatta"),[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandra2005-70) which were chosen as the site of impressive [Mughal buildings](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalik200679-71) In the early 16th century, the region remained under the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006-72) In the 18th century, the slow disintegration of the Mughal Empire was hastened by the emergence of the rival powers like the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") and later the [Sikh Empire](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire"), as well as invasions by [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah") from Iran in 1739 and the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") of Afghanistan in 1759.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaleem2013-12)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald2017-73) The growing political power of the British in Bengal had not yet reached the territories of modern Pakistan.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimpson2007-74)

### Colonial rule

Main articles: [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), [Aligarh Movement](/wiki/Aligarh_Movement "Aligarh Movement"), and [Two-nation theory](/wiki/Two-nation_theory "Two-nation theory")

[![Sir Syed Ahmad Khan (1817–1898), whose vision (Two-nation theory) formed the basis of Pakistan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1a/Sir_Syed1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Syed1.jpg)

Sir [Syed Ahmad Khan](/wiki/Syed_Ahmad_Khan "Syed Ahmad Khan") (1817–1898), whose [vision](/wiki/Two-nation_theory "Two-nation theory") formed the basis of Pakistan.[[67]](#cite_note-Wolpert-1984-75)[[68]](#cite_note-Sengupta-2023-76)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoltCurta2016-77)

[![Muhammad Ali Jinnah (1876–1948) served as Pakistan's first Governor-General and the leader of the Pakistan Movement](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Jinnah1945b.jpg/250px-Jinnah1945b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jinnah1945b.jpg)

[Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") (1876–1948) served as Pakistan's first Governor-General and the leader of the [Pakistan Movement](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert1984-78)

None of modern Pakistan was under British rule until 1839 when [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), a small fishing village governed by [Talpurs](/wiki/Talpur_dynasty "Talpur dynasty") of [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") with a mud fort guarding the harbour, was [taken](/wiki/History_of_Karachi#Company_rule "History of Karachi"),[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERustomji1952-79)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalbridge2012-80) and used as an enclave with a port and [military base](/wiki/Karachi_Cantonment "Karachi Cantonment") for the [First Afghan War](/wiki/First_Afghan_War "First Afghan War") that ensued.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGayer2014-81) The remainder of [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") was acquired in 1843,[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharmaD'AngeloGiri2020-82) and subsequently, through a series of wars and treaties, the [East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"), and later, after the post-[Sepoy Mutiny](/wiki/Sepoy_Mutiny "Sepoy Mutiny") (1857–1858), direct rule by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), acquired most of the region.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPirbhai2009-83) Key conflicts included those against the [Baloch](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") [Talpur dynasty](/wiki/Talpur_dynasty "Talpur dynasty"), resolved by the [Battle of Miani](/wiki/Battle_of_Miani "Battle of Miani") (1843) in Sindh,[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarjani2018-84) the [Anglo-Sikh Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Sikh_War_(disambiguation) "Anglo-Sikh War (disambiguation)") (1845–1849),[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook1975-85) and the [Anglo–Afghan Wars](/w/index.php?title=Anglo%E2%80%93Afghan_War&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anglo–Afghan War (page does not exist)") (1839–1919).[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2022119-86) By 1893, all modern Pakistan was part of the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire"), and remained so until independence in 1947.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavendish2006365-87)

Under British rule, modern Pakistan was primarily divided into the [Sind Division](/wiki/Sind_Division "Sind Division"), [Punjab Province](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)"), and the [Baluchistan Agency](/wiki/Baluchistan_Agency "Baluchistan Agency"). The region also included various [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"), with the largest being [Bahawalpur](/wiki/Bahawalpur_(princely_state) "Bahawalpur (princely state)").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaw1999-88)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHussain2015-89)

An important uprising against the British in the region was the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), known at the time as the [Sepoy Mutiny](/wiki/Sepoy_Mutiny "Sepoy Mutiny").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENyrop1975371-90) Divergence in the [relationship](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Islamic_relations "Hindu–Islamic relations") between [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and Islam resulted in significant tension in [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), leading to religious violence. The [language controversy](/wiki/Hindi%E2%80%93Urdu_controversy "Hindi–Urdu controversy") further exacerbated tensions between Hindus and Muslims.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoltCurta2016-77)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaliAkhtar1993-91) A [Muslim intellectual movement](/wiki/Aligarh_Movement "Aligarh Movement"), led by Sir [Syed Ahmed Khan](/wiki/Syed_Ahmed_Khan "Syed Ahmed Khan") to counter the [Hindu renaissance](/wiki/Bengali_Renaissance "Bengali Renaissance"), advocated for the [two-nation theory](/wiki/Two-nation_theory "Two-nation theory") and led to the establishment of the [All-India Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League") in 1906.[[67]](#cite_note-Wolpert-1984-75)[[68]](#cite_note-Sengupta-2023-76)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoltCurta2016-77)

In March 1929, in response to the [Nehru Report](/wiki/Nehru_Report "Nehru Report"), [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah"), the founder of Pakistan, issued his [fourteen points](/wiki/Fourteen_Points_of_Jinnah "Fourteen Points of Jinnah"), which included proposals to safeguard the interests of the Muslim minority in a united India. These proposals were rejected.[[84]](#cite_note-Hardy-1972-92)[[85]](#cite_note-Wuthnow-2013-93)[[86]](#cite_note-Singh-Shani-2021-94) In his 29 December 1930 address, [Allama Iqbal](/wiki/Muhammad_Iqbal "Muhammad Iqbal") advocated the amalgamation of Muslim-majority states in [North-West](/wiki/Northwest_India_(pre-1947) "Northwest India (pre-1947)") India, including [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)"), [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%9355) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–55)"), [Sind](/wiki/Sind_Province_(1936%E2%80%9355) "Sind Province (1936–55)"), and [Baluchistan](/wiki/Baluchistan_(Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province) "Baluchistan (Chief Commissioner's Province)").[[86]](#cite_note-Singh-Shani-2021-94)[[87]](#cite_note-Iqbal-95) The perception that Congress-led British [provincial governments](/wiki/1937_Indian_provincial_elections "1937 Indian provincial elections") neglected the Muslim League from 1937 to 1939 motivated Jinnah and other Muslim League leaders to embrace the two-nation theory.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPandeya2003-96)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasuMiroshnik2017-97) This led to the adoption of the [Lahore Resolution](/wiki/Lahore_Resolution "Lahore Resolution") of 1940, presented by [Sher-e-Bangla](/wiki/Sher-e-Bangla "Sher-e-Bangla") [A.K. Fazlul Haque](/wiki/A.K._Fazlul_Haque "A.K. Fazlul Haque"), also known as the Pakistan Resolution.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEM._H._Khan2016-98)

By 1942, Britain faced considerable strain during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), with India directly threatened by Japanese forces. Britain had pledged voluntary independence for India in exchange for support during the war. However, this pledge included a clause stating that no part of British India would be compelled to join the resulting dominion, which could be interpreted as support for an independent Muslim nation. Congress under the leadership of [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") launched the [Quit India Movement](/wiki/Quit_India_Movement "Quit India Movement"), demanding an immediate end to British rule. In contrast, the Muslim League chose to support the [UK's war efforts](/wiki/United_Kingdom_in_World_War_II "United Kingdom in World War II"), thereby nurturing the possibility of establishing a Muslim nation.[[91]](#cite_note-Tucker-2020-99)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandra2008-100)

### Independence

Main article: [Pakistan Movement](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement")

Further information: [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") and [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Partition_of_India_1947_en.svg/330px-Partition_of_India_1947_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Partition_of_India_1947_en.svg)

The [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"): green regions were all part of Pakistan by 1948, and orange ones part of India. The darker-shaded regions represent the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") and [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") provinces partitioned by the [Radcliffe Line](/wiki/Radcliffe_Line "Radcliffe Line"). The grey areas represent some of the key [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") that were eventually integrated into India or Pakistan.

The [1946 elections](/wiki/1946_Indian_provincial_elections "1946 Indian provincial elections") saw the Muslim League secure 90 percent of the Muslim seats, supported by the landowners of Sindh and Punjab. This forced the Indian National Congress, initially skeptical of the League's representation of Indian Muslims, to acknowledge its significance.[[93]](#cite_note-Mohiuddin-2007-1-101) Jinnah's emergence as the voice of the Indian Muslims,[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoodbhoy2023-61) compelled the British to consider their stance, despite their [reluctance to partition India](/wiki/Opposition_to_the_partition_of_India "Opposition to the partition of India"). In a final attempt to prevent partition, they proposed the [Cabinet Mission Plan](/wiki/Cabinet_Mission_Plan "Cabinet Mission Plan").[[94]](#cite_note-Mohiuddin-2007-2-102)

As the Cabinet Mission failed, the British announced their intention to end rule by June 1948.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert1984309-103)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkovits2012-104) Following rigorous discussions involving [Viceroy of India](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India"), [Lord Mountbatten of Burma](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten,_1st_Earl_Mountbatten_of_Burma "Louis Mountbatten, 1st Earl Mountbatten of Burma"), [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") of the [All-India Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League"), and [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru") of Congress, the formal declaration to partition British India into two independent dominions—namely Pakistan and India—was issued by Mountbatten on the evening of 3 June 1947. In Mountbatten's oval office, the prime ministers of around a dozen major princely states gathered to receive their copies of the plan before its worldwide broadcast. At 7:00 P.M., [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio") transmitted the public announcement, starting with the viceroy's address, followed by individual speeches from Nehru, and Jinnah. The founder of Pakistan [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") concluded his address with the slogan *Pakistan Zindabad* (Long Live Pakistan).[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert1984328–329-105)

As the United Kingdom agreed to the [partitioning of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"),[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert1984328–329-105) the modern state of Pakistan was established on [14 August 1947](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Pakistan) "Independence Day (Pakistan)") (27th of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan") in 1366 of the [Islamic calendar](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar"), [a holy time regarded as auspicious and noted for its religious importance](/wiki/Night_of_Power "Night of Power")).[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHakeem2014-106)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENDTV2025-107) This new nation amalgamated the [Muslim-majority](/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India") eastern and [northwestern regions](/wiki/Northwest_India_(pre-1947) "Northwest India (pre-1947)") of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), comprising the provinces of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan_(Pakistan) "Balochistan (Pakistan)"), [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"), the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)"), [West Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), and Sindh.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen20046-108)

In the riots that accompanied the partition in Punjab Province, between 200,000 and 2,000,000 people were killed in what some have described as a retributive genocide between the religions.[[101]](#cite_note-Riots-1-109) Around 50,000 Muslim women were [abducted and raped](/wiki/Violence_against_women_during_the_partition_of_India "Violence against women during the partition of India") by Hindu and Sikh men, while 33,000 Hindu and Sikh women experienced the same fate at the hands of Muslims.[[102]](#cite_note-Riots-2-110) Around 6.5 million Muslims moved from India to West Pakistan and 4.7 million Hindus and Sikhs moved from West Pakistan to India.[[103]](#cite_note-Hasan-Raza-2009-111) It was the largest mass migration in human history.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiggs2024-112) A subsequent dispute over the [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)") eventually sparked the [Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1947%E2%80%931948 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1947–1948").[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhaumik1996-113)

### Post-independence

Main articles: [History of Pakistan (1947–present)](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan_(1947%E2%80%93present) "History of Pakistan (1947–present)") and [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Liaquat_Ali_Khan_1945.jpg/250px-Liaquat_Ali_Khan_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liaquat_Ali_Khan_1945.jpg)

[Liaquat Ali Khan](/wiki/Liaquat_Ali_Khan "Liaquat Ali Khan") was elected the first Prime Minister of Pakistan.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKazmi2003-114)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2017-115)

After [independence](/wiki/Creation_of_Pakistan "Creation of Pakistan") in 1947, Jinnah, the [President](/wiki/Party_chair "Party chair") of the Muslim League, became Pakistan's first [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Pakistan "Governor-General of Pakistan") and the first [President-Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "Speaker of the National Assembly of Pakistan") of the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Pakistan "Parliament of Pakistan"), but he died of tuberculosis on 11 September 1948.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2017-115)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkbar2018-116) Meanwhile, Pakistan's founding fathers agreed to appoint [Liaquat Ali Khan](/wiki/Liaquat_Ali_Khan "Liaquat Ali Khan"), the [secretary-general](/wiki/Secretary_General "Secretary General") of the [party](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League"), the nation's [first Prime Minister](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_Pakistan "List of Prime Ministers of Pakistan").[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKazmi2003-114)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2017-115) From 1947 to 1956, [Pakistan was a monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Pakistan "Monarchy of Pakistan") within the Commonwealth of Nations, and had two monarchs before it became a republic.[[109]](#cite_note-Kumarasingham-2013-117)

The creation of Pakistan was never fully accepted by many British leaders including [Lord Mountbatten](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten,_1st_Earl_Mountbatten_of_Burma "Louis Mountbatten, 1st Earl Mountbatten of Burma").[[110]](#cite_note-McGrath-1996-118) Mountbatten expressed his lack of support and faith in the Muslim League's idea of Pakistan.[[111]](#cite_note-Ahmed-1997-119) Jinnah refused Mountbatten's offer to serve as Governor-General of Pakistan.[[112]](#cite_note-Wolpert-2009-120) When Mountbatten was asked by [Collins](/wiki/Larry_Collins_(writer) "Larry Collins (writer)") and [Lapierre](/wiki/Dominique_Lapierre "Dominique Lapierre") if he would have sabotaged Pakistan had he known that Jinnah was dying of tuberculosis, he replied 'most probably'.[[113]](#cite_note-Lapierre-Collins-2015-121)

The American [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") film on Pakistan, made in 1950, examines the history and geography of Pakistan.

> "You are free; you are free to go to your temples, you are free to go to your mosques or to any other place of worship in this State of Pakistan. You may belong to any religion or caste or creed – that has nothing to do with the business of the State."

—[Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah")'s first speech to the Constituent Assembly of Pakistan.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2009-122)

Maulana [Shabbir Ahmad Usmani](/wiki/Shabbir_Ahmad_Usmani "Shabbir Ahmad Usmani"), a respected Deobandi *alim* (scholar) who held the position of Shaykh al-Islam in Pakistan in 1949, and [Maulana Mawdudi](/wiki/Abul_A%27la_Maududi "Abul A'la Maududi") of [Jamaat-i-Islami](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami "Jamaat-e-Islami") played key roles in advocating for an Islamic constitution. Mawdudi insisted that the Constituent Assembly declare the "supreme sovereignty of God" and the supremacy of the *shariah* in Pakistan.[[115]](#cite_note-Hussain-2008-1-123)

The efforts of Jamaat-i-Islami and the *[ulama](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama")* led to the passage of the [Objectives Resolution](/wiki/Objectives_Resolution "Objectives Resolution") in March 1949. This resolution, described by Liaquat Ali Khan as the second most significant step in Pakistan's history, affirmed that "sovereignty over the entire universe belongs to God Almighty alone and the authority which He has delegated to the State of Pakistan through its people for being exercised within the limits prescribed by Him is a sacred trust". It was later included as a preamble to the constitutions of 1956, 1962, and 1973.[[116]](#cite_note-Hussain-2008-2-124)

[Democracy](/wiki/Democracy_in_Pakistan "Democracy in Pakistan") faced setbacks due to the [martial law](/wiki/1958_Pakistani_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1958 Pakistani coup d'état") imposed by President [Iskander Mirza](/wiki/Iskander_Mirza "Iskander Mirza"), who was succeeded by General [Ayub Khan](/wiki/Ayub_Khan_(President_of_Pakistan) "Ayub Khan (President of Pakistan)"). After adopting a [presidential system](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1962 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1962") in 1962, Pakistan witnessed significant growth until the [second war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1965") with India in 1965, resulting in an economic downturn and widespread public discontent in 1967.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynbrandt2009190–197-125)[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChowdhuryMahmud2008-126) In 1969, President [Yahya Khan](/wiki/Yahya_Khan "Yahya Khan") consolidated control, but faced a devastating [cyclone](/wiki/1970_Bhola_cyclone "1970 Bhola cyclone") in East Pakistan resulting in 500,000 deaths.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKathpalia1986-127)

In 1970, Pakistan conducted its [first democratic elections](/wiki/1970_Pakistani_general_election "1970 Pakistani general election") since independence, intending to transition from [military rule](/wiki/Legal_Framework_Order,_1970 "Legal Framework Order, 1970") to democracy. However, after the East Pakistani [Awami League](/wiki/Awami_League "Awami League") emerged victorious over the [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") (PPP), Yahya Khan and the military refused to transfer power.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoumar2023-128) This led to [Operation Searchlight](/wiki/Operation_Searchlight "Operation Searchlight"), a military crackdown, and eventually sparked the [war of liberation](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War") by Bengali [Mukti Bahini](/wiki/Mukti_Bahini "Mukti Bahini") forces in East Pakistan,[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis2011-129) described in West Pakistan as a civil war rather than a liberation struggle.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBose2005-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Ayubkhanandbhutto.jpg/250px-Ayubkhanandbhutto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayubkhanandbhutto.jpg)

Signing of the [Tashkent Declaration](/wiki/Tashkent_Declaration "Tashkent Declaration") to end [hostilities](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1965") with India in 1965 in [Tashkent](/wiki/Tashkent "Tashkent"), [USSR](/wiki/Union_of_Soviet_Socialist_Republics_(USSR) "Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR)"), by President [Ayub](/wiki/Field_Marshal_Ayub_Khan "Field Marshal Ayub Khan") alongside [Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto") (centre) and [Aziz Ahmed](/wiki/Aziz_Ahmed_(civil_servant) "Aziz Ahmed (civil servant)") (left)[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2008-131)

Independent researchers estimate that between 300,000 and 500,000 civilians died during this period while the Bangladesh government puts the number of dead at three million,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunkaraWalterRojas2024-132) a figure that is now nearly universally regarded as excessively inflated.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiro2015-133) Some academics such as [Rudolph Rummel](/wiki/Rudolph_Rummel "Rudolph Rummel") and [Rounaq Jahan](/wiki/Rounaq_Jahan "Rounaq Jahan") say both sides committed genocide;[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERummel1998-134) others such as [Richard Sisson](/wiki/John_Richard_Sisson "John Richard Sisson") and Leo E. Rose believe there was no genocide.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeachler2011-135) In response to India's support for the insurgency in East Pakistan, [preemptive strikes](/wiki/Preemptive_strikes "Preemptive strikes") on India by Pakistan's [air force](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force"), [navy](/wiki/Pakistan_Navy "Pakistan Navy"), and [marines](/wiki/Pakistan_Marines "Pakistan Marines") sparked [a conventional war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971") in 1971 that resulted in an Indian victory and East Pakistan gaining [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Bangladesh "Independence of Bangladesh") as [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETotten2000-136)

Yahya Khan was replaced by [Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto") as president; the country worked towards promulgating [its constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan") and putting the country on the road to democracy.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaxton2016-137)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOldenburg2010-138) In 1972 Pakistan embarked on an ambitious plan to develop its [nuclear deterrence](/wiki/Pakistan_and_its_Nuclear_Deterrent_Program "Pakistan and its Nuclear Deterrent Program") capability with [the goal of preventing](/wiki/Mutually_assured_destruction "Mutually assured destruction") any [foreign invasion](/wiki/Foreign_interventionism "Foreign interventionism"); the country's [first](/wiki/KANUPP "KANUPP") [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") was inaugurated in that same year.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFitzpatrick2007-139)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoodbhoy2011-140) India's [first nuclear test](/wiki/Operation_Smiling_Buddha "Operation Smiling Buddha") in 1974 gave Pakistan additional justification to accelerate its [nuclear program](/wiki/Pakistan_and_Nuclear_Weapons "Pakistan and Nuclear Weapons").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoodbhoy2011-140)

Democracy ended with a [military coup](/wiki/Operation_Fair_Play "Operation Fair Play") in 1977 against the [leftist](/wiki/Socialism_in_Pakistan "Socialism in Pakistan") PPP, which saw [General Zia-ul-Haq](/wiki/General_Zia-ul-Haq "General Zia-ul-Haq") become the president in 1978.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrasnoLaPides2015-141) From 1977 to 1988, President Zia's [corporatisation](/wiki/Corporate_sector_of_Pakistan "Corporate sector of Pakistan") and [economic Islamisation](/wiki/Islamization_of_Economy "Islamization of Economy") initiatives led to Pakistan becoming one of the fastest-growing economies in South Asia.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhanna2002-142) While building up the country's [nuclear program](/wiki/Pakistan_and_its_Nuclear_Deterrent_Program "Pakistan and its Nuclear Deterrent Program"), increasing [Islamisation](/wiki/Zia-ul-Haq%27s_Islamization "Zia-ul-Haq's Islamization"), and the rise of a homegrown [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Pakistan "Conservatism in Pakistan") philosophy, Pakistan helped subsidise and distribute US [resources to factions](/wiki/Operation_Cyclone "Operation Cyclone") of the [mujahideen](/wiki/Afghan_mujahideen "Afghan mujahideen") against the [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")'s [intervention](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") in [communist Afghanistan](/wiki/Communist_Afghanistan "Communist Afghanistan").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHajari2015-143)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColl2004-144)[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestad2005-145) Pakistan's [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") became a base for the anti-Soviet Afghan fighters, with the province's influential [Deobandi](/wiki/Deobandi "Deobandi") ulama playing a significant role in encouraging and organising the 'jihad'.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon2008-146)

[President Zia died](/wiki/Death_of_Muhammad_Zia-ul-Haq "Death of Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq") in a plane crash in 1988, and [Benazir Bhutto](/wiki/Benazir_Bhutto "Benazir Bhutto"), daughter of Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, was elected as the country's [first female](/wiki/Women_in_Muslim_societies "Women in Muslim societies") Prime Minister. The PPP was followed by conservative [Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)") (PML (N)), and over the next decade the leaders of the two parties fought for power, alternating in office.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2015-147) This period is marked by prolonged [stagflation](/wiki/Periods_of_stagflation_in_Pakistan "Periods of stagflation in Pakistan"), political instability, [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Pakistan "Corruption in Pakistan"), misgovernment, [geopolitical](/wiki/Geostrategy "Geostrategy") rivalry with India, and the clash of [left wing](/wiki/Socialism_in_Pakistan "Socialism in Pakistan")-[right wing](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Pakistan "Conservatism in Pakistan") ideologies.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChapman2018-148)[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHusain2010-149) As PML (N) secured a [supermajority](/wiki/Supermajority "Supermajority") in [elections](/wiki/1997_Pakistani_general_election "1997 Pakistani general election") in 1997,[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYapAbeyratne202368-150) [Nawaz Sharif](/wiki/Nawaz_Sharif "Nawaz Sharif") authorised [nuclear testings](/wiki/List_of_nuclear_weapons_tests_of_Pakistan "List of nuclear weapons tests of Pakistan"), as a [retaliation](/wiki/Tit-for-tat "Tit-for-tat") to the [second nuclear tests](/wiki/Pokhran-II "Pokhran-II") conducted by India in May 1998.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2012-151)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/The_Prime_Minister_Shri_Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee_meets_the_President_of_Pakistan_Mr._Pervez_Musharraf_on_the_sidline_of_12th_SAARC_Summit_in_Islmabad_on_January_5%2C_2003.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Prime_Minister_Shri_Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee_meets_the_President_of_Pakistan_Mr._Pervez_Musharraf_on_the_sidline_of_12th_SAARC_Summit_in_Islmabad_on_January_5,_2003.jpg)

President [Musharraf](/wiki/Pervez_Musharraf "Pervez Musharraf") meets with Indian Prime Minister [Vajpayee](/wiki/Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee "Atal Bihari Vajpayee") in [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad") at the sidelines of 12th [SAARC](/wiki/SAARC "SAARC") summit in 2004.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmad2023-152)

Military tension between the two countries in the [Kargil district](/wiki/Kargil_district "Kargil district") led to the [Kargil War](/wiki/Kargil_War "Kargil War") of 1999,[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMazari2003-153)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChakma2014-154) and turmoil in [civil-military relations](/wiki/Civil-military_relations "Civil-military relations") allowed General [Pervez Musharraf](/wiki/Pervez_Musharraf "Pervez Musharraf") to take over through a [bloodless coup d'état](/wiki/1999_Pakistani_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1999 Pakistani coup d'état").[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYarbakhsh2019-155) Musharraf governed Pakistan as [chief executive](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") from 1999 to 2002 and as president from 2001 to 2008[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhoja-Moolji2021-156)—a period of [enlightenment](/wiki/Enlightened_moderation "Enlightened moderation"),[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFair2014-157)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2021-158) social [liberalism](/wiki/Liberal_islam "Liberal islam"),[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbid2023-159) extensive [economic reforms](/wiki/Economic_reforms_in_Pakistan "Economic reforms in Pakistan"),[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMohiuddin2007219-160) and [direct involvement in the US-led war on terrorism](/wiki/Pakistan%27s_role_in_the_War_on_Terror "Pakistan's role in the War on Terror").[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2021-158) By its own financial calculations, Pakistan's [involvement in the war on terrorism](/wiki/Pakistan%27s_role_in_the_War_on_Terror "Pakistan's role in the War on Terror") has cost up to $118 billion, over [eighty one thousand casualties](/wiki/List_of_terrorist_incidents_in_Pakistan_since_2001 "List of terrorist incidents in Pakistan since 2001"),[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartiniFordJackson2020-161) and more than 1.8 million displaced civilians.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMansbachPirroTaylor2017-162)

The [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Pakistan) "National Assembly (Pakistan)") historically completed its first full five-year term on 15 November 2007.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnited_States_Senate_Committee_on_Foreign_Relations2008-163) After the [assassination of Benazir Bhutto](/wiki/Assassination_of_Benazir_Bhutto "Assassination of Benazir Bhutto") in 2007, the PPP secured the [most votes](/wiki/Majority "Majority") in the [elections](/wiki/2008_Pakistani_general_election "2008 Pakistani general election") of 2008, appointing party member [Yusuf Raza Gilani](/wiki/Yusuf_Raza_Gilani "Yusuf Raza Gilani") as Prime Minister.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaffrelot2015261-164) Threatened with [impeachment](/wiki/Movement_to_impeach_Pervez_Musharraf "Movement to impeach Pervez Musharraf"), President Musharraf resigned on 18 August 2008, and was succeeded by [Asif Ali Zardari](/wiki/Asif_Ali_Zardari "Asif Ali Zardari").[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKapoor2009-165) Clashes with the [judicature](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Pakistan "Judiciary of Pakistan") prompted Gilani's disqualification from the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Pakistan "Parliament of Pakistan") and as the Prime Minister in June 2012.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaseem2022-166) The [general election](/wiki/2013_Pakistani_general_election "2013 Pakistani general election") held in 2013 saw the PML (N) achieve victory,[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDedeSadioglu2016-167) following which Nawaz Sharif was elected as Prime Minister for the third time.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERuhland2019-168) In 2018, [PTI](/wiki/Pakistan_Tehreek-e-Insaf "Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf") won the [general election](/wiki/2018_Pakistan_general_election "2018 Pakistan general election") and [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan") became the 22nd Prime Minister.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurnett2020-169) In April 2022, [Shehbaz Sharif](/wiki/Shehbaz_Sharif "Shehbaz Sharif") was elected as prime minister, after Imran Khan lost a no-confidence vote.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECentral_Intelligence_Agency2023-170) During [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_Pakistani_general_election "2024 Pakistani general election"), PTI-backed independents became the largest bloc,[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAfzal2024-171) but Shehbaz Sharif was elected prime minister for a second term, as a result of a coalition between PML (N) and PPPP.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETariqStenson2024-172)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Pakistan](/wiki/Geography_of_Pakistan "Geography of Pakistan"), [Environment of Pakistan](/wiki/Environment_of_Pakistan "Environment of Pakistan"), [Climate of Pakistan](/wiki/Climate_of_Pakistan "Climate of Pakistan"), [Extreme weather records in Pakistan](/wiki/List_of_extreme_weather_records_in_Pakistan "List of extreme weather records in Pakistan"), [List of tropical cyclones in Pakistan](/wiki/List_of_tropical_cyclones_in_Pakistan "List of tropical cyclones in Pakistan"), and [List of beaches in Pakistan](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_Pakistan "List of beaches in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Koppen-Geiger_Map_PAK_present.svg/500px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_PAK_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_PAK_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Pakistan

Pakistan's diverse [geography](/wiki/Geography_of_Pakistan "Geography of Pakistan") and [climate](/wiki/Climate_of_Pakistan "Climate of Pakistan") host a wide array of [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Pakistan "Wildlife of Pakistan").[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECheng_et_al.2022-173) Covering 881,913 km2 (340,509 sq mi),[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAgarwalAhmad2021-174) Pakistan ranks as the [33rd-largest nation by total area](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_area "List of countries by area"),[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMordiAdisa2022-175) but this varies based on Kashmir's disputed status. Pakistan boasts a 1,046 km (650 mi) coastline along the Arabian Sea and the Gulf of Oman,[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaque2002-176)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBritannica_(Gulf_of_Oman)2024-177) and shares land borders totaling 6,774 km (4,209 mi), including 2,430 km (1,510 mi) [with Afghanistan](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line"), 523 km (325 mi) [with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Pakistan_border "China–Pakistan border"), 2,912 km (1,809 mi) [with India](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Pakistan_border "India–Pakistan border"), and 909 km (565 mi) [with Iran](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Pakistan_border "Iran–Pakistan border").[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDawn2009-178) It has a [maritime border with Oman](/wiki/Oman%E2%80%93Pakistan_maritime_boundary "Oman–Pakistan maritime boundary")[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaraman2012-179) and is separated from Tajikistan via the narrow strip of the [Wakhan Corridor](/wiki/Wakhan_Corridor "Wakhan Corridor").[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanerjee2019-180) Situated at the crossroads of South Asia, the Middle East, and Central Asia,[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMohiuddin20073,_317,_323–324-181) Pakistan's location is geopolitically significant.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKreft2007-182) Geologically, Pakistan lies at the interaction of the [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Plate "Indian Plate") and [Eurasian tectonic plates](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") within the Indus–Tsangpo Suture Zone, a region of high seismic activity. The collision between these two plates occurs in [northwest Pakistan](/wiki/Northwest_Pakistan "Northwest Pakistan"), particularly in [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"). Sindh and Punjab are located on the north-western corner of the Indian Plate, while Balochistan and much of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa lie along its north-western margin. [Northern areas of Pakistan](/wiki/Northern_Pakistan "Northern Pakistan"), including Azad Kashmir, lie along the northern edge of the Indian Plate and are [prone to powerful earthquakes](/wiki/List_of_earthquakes_in_Pakistan "List of earthquakes in Pakistan").[[175]](#cite_note-Geology-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Indus.A2002274.0610.1km.jpg/250px-Indus.A2002274.0610.1km.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indus.A2002274.0610.1km.jpg)

A satellite image showing the topography of Pakistan[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDescloitres2002-184)

Pakistan's landscapes vary from coastal plains to glaciated mountains, offering deserts, forests, hills, and plateaus.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavendish2006297-185) Pakistan is divided into three major geographic areas: the northern highlands, the Indus River plain, and the Balochistan Plateau.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood199682-186) The northern highlands feature the [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram"), [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush"), and [Pamir](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") mountain ranges, hosting some of the world's highest peaks, including five of the fourteen [eight-thousanders](/wiki/Eight-thousanders "Eight-thousanders") (mountain peaks over 8,000 metres or 26,250 feet), notably [K2](/wiki/K2 "K2") (8,611 m or 28,251 ft) and [Nanga Parbat](/wiki/Nanga_Parbat "Nanga Parbat") (8,126 m or 26,660 ft).[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJiwani2021-187)[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBright2017-188) The Balochistan Plateau lies in the west and the [Thar Desert](/wiki/Thar_Desert "Thar Desert") in the east.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood199683-189)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmad2009-190)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHasanRaza200910-191) The 1,609 km (1,000 mi) Indus River and its tributaries traverse the nation from Kashmir to the Arabian Sea, sustaining alluvial plains along the Punjab and Sindh regions.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel2016-192)

The climate varies from tropical to temperate, with arid conditions in the coastal south. There is a monsoon season with frequent flooding due to heavy rainfall, and a dry season with significantly less rainfall or none at all.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandrappaGuptaKulshrestha2011-193) Pakistan experiences four distinct seasons: a cool, dry winter from December through February; a hot, dry spring from March through May; the summer rainy season, or southwest monsoon period, from June through September; and the retreating monsoon period of October and November.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood199687-194) Rainfall varies greatly from year to year, with patterns of alternate flooding and drought common.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaneNortonRyan2017-195)

### Flora and fauna

Main articles: [Wildlife of Pakistan](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Pakistan "Wildlife of Pakistan"), [Flora of Pakistan](/wiki/Flora_of_Pakistan "Flora of Pakistan"), and [Fauna of Pakistan](/wiki/Fauna_of_Pakistan "Fauna of Pakistan")

The diverse landscape and climate in Pakistan support a wide range of trees and plants.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEl-Esawi2019-196) From coniferous [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_plant "Alpine plant") and [subalpine](/wiki/Subalpine "Subalpine") trees like [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce"), [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine"), and [deodar cedar](/wiki/Deodar_cedar "Deodar cedar") in the northern mountains to [deciduous trees](/wiki/Deciduous_trees "Deciduous trees") like [shisham](/wiki/Shisham "Shisham") in the [Sulaiman Mountains](/wiki/Sulaiman_Mountains "Sulaiman Mountains"),[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandrappaGuptaKulshrestha2011-193) and palms such as [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut") and [date](/wiki/Phoenix_dactylifera "Phoenix dactylifera") in the southern regions.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbul-Soad2011-197)[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDescals_et_al.2023-198) The western hills boast [juniper](/wiki/Juniper "Juniper"), [tamarisk](/wiki/Tamarisk "Tamarisk"), coarse grasses, and scrub plants.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpateLearmonth2017-199) [Mangrove](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") forests dominate the coastal wetlands in the south.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandhu2010-200) Coniferous forests span altitudes from 1,000 to 4,000 metres (3,300 to 13,100 feet) in most northern and northwestern highlands.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUNEP-WCMC2024-201) In Balochistan's xeric regions, date palms and *[Ephedra](/wiki/Ephedra_(genus) "Ephedra (genus)")* are prevalent.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbul-Soad2011-197)[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkhtarMirza2006-202) In Punjab and Sindh's Indus plains, tropical and subtropical dry and moist broadleaf forests as well as tropical and xeric shrublands thrive.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPEPA2016-203) Approximately 4.8% or 36,845.6 square kilometres (3,684,560 ha) of Pakistan was forested in 2021.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank2024-204)[[i]](#cite_note-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Markhor_Horns_%285779055412%29.jpg/250px-Markhor_Horns_%285779055412%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Markhor_Horns_(5779055412).jpg)

[Markhor](/wiki/Markhor "Markhor") is the national animal of Pakistan.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFatima2020-206)

Pakistan's fauna mirrors its diverse climate. The country boasts around 668 bird species,[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaridah-HanumHakeemÖztürkEfe2015-207) including [crows](/wiki/Crow "Crow"), [sparrows](/wiki/Old_World_sparrow "Old World sparrow"), [mynas](/wiki/Acridotheres "Acridotheres"), [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk"), [falcons](/wiki/Falcon "Falcon"), and [eagles](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle"). [Palas, Kohistan](/wiki/Palas,_Kohistan "Palas, Kohistan"), is home to the [western tragopan](/wiki/Western_tragopan "Western tragopan"), with many migratory birds visiting from Europe, Central Asia, and India.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimmettInskipp2021-208) The southern plains harbor [mongooses](/wiki/Mongoose "Mongoose"),[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter2018-209) [small Indian civet](/wiki/Small_Indian_civet "Small Indian civet"),[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanBelantSatoSomers2021-210) hares,[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFluxChapman1990-211) the [Asiatic jackal](/wiki/Asiatic_jackal "Asiatic jackal"),[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESrinivasuluSrinivasulu2012-212) the [Indian pangolin](/wiki/Indian_pangolin "Indian pangolin"),[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaseem_et_al.2020-213) the [jungle cat](/wiki/Jungle_cat "Jungle cat"),[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunquistSunquist2014-214) and the [sand cat](/wiki/Sand_cat "Sand cat").[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunquistSunquist2017-215) Indus is home to [mugger crocodiles](/wiki/Mugger_crocodile "Mugger crocodile"),[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStoneman2021-216) while surrounding areas host [wild boars](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"),[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETisdell2013-217) deer,[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESrinivasulu2018-218) and [porcupines](/wiki/Indian_porcupine "Indian porcupine").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoze2012-219) Central Pakistan's sandy scrublands shelter Asiatic jackals,[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESrinivasuluSrinivasulu2012-212) [striped hyenas](/wiki/Striped_hyena "Striped hyena"),[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESomerville2021-220) wildcats, and [leopards](/wiki/Leopard "Leopard"). The mountainous north hosts a variety of animals like the [Marco Polo sheep](/wiki/Marco_Polo_sheep "Marco Polo sheep"),[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENyrop197574-221) [urial](/wiki/Urial "Urial"), [markhor](/wiki/Markhor "Markhor") goat, [ibex](/wiki/Ibex "Ibex") goat, [Asian black bear](/wiki/Asian_black_bear "Asian black bear"), and [Himalayan brown bear](/wiki/Himalayan_brown_bear "Himalayan brown bear").[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandrappaGuptaKulshrestha2011-193)

The lack of vegetative cover, severe climate, and grazing impact on deserts have endangered wild animals.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECBD_Report2009-222) The [chinkara](/wiki/Chinkara "Chinkara") is the only animal found in significant numbers in [Cholistan](/wiki/Cholistan "Cholistan"),[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMallonKingswood2001-223) with a few [nilgai](/wiki/Nilgai "Nilgai") along the Pakistan–India border and in some parts of Cholistan.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodsMuftiHasan1997-224) Rare animals include the [snow leopard](/wiki/Snow_leopard "Snow leopard") and the blind [Indus river dolphin](/wiki/Indus_river_dolphin "Indus river dolphin"),[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandrappaGuptaKulshrestha2011-193) of which there are believed to be about 1,816 remaining, protected at the [Indus Dolphin Reserve](/wiki/Indus_Dolphin_Reserve "Indus Dolphin Reserve") in Sindh.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWWF2024-225) In total, 174 species of mammals, 177 species of reptiles, 22 species of amphibians, 198 species of freshwater fish, 668 species of birds, over 5,000 species of insects, and over 5,700 species of plants have been recorded in Pakistan.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaridah-HanumHakeemÖztürkEfe2015-207) Pakistan faces deforestation, hunting, and pollution, with a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 7.42/10, ranking 41st globally out of 172 countries.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrantham_et_al.2020-226)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Pakistan](/wiki/Government_of_Pakistan "Government of Pakistan"), [Politics of Pakistan](/wiki/Politics_of_Pakistan "Politics of Pakistan"), and [Elections in Pakistan](/wiki/Elections_in_Pakistan "Elections in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Parliament_House%2C_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg/250px-Parliament_House%2C_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parliament_House,_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg)

[Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_of_Pakistan "Parliament of Pakistan")

Pakistan operates as a democratic [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") [federal republic](/wiki/Federal_republic "Federal republic"), with Islam designated as the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion").[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInter-Parliamentary_Union1973-227)[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMunir1975-228) The military's influence in mainstream politics has been significant throughout Pakistan's history.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen2004-229) Initially adopting a [constitution in 1956](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1956 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1956"), Pakistan saw it suspended by Ayub Khan in 1958, replaced by a [second constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1962 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1962") in 1962.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen200465-230) Under the leadership of [Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto"), the [1973 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan") established a parliamentary system, but it was suspended under [Zia-ul-Haq](/wiki/Zia-ul-Haq "Zia-ul-Haq")'s martial law in 1977 and restored, with amendments, in 1985.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeVotta2015-231) The eras of 1958–1971, [1977–1988](/wiki/1977_Pakistani_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1977 Pakistani coup d'état"), and 1999–2008 witnessed [military coups](/wiki/Military_coups_in_Pakistan "Military coups in Pakistan"), leading to [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Pakistan "Martial law in Pakistan") and military leaders governing de facto as presidents.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETertraisSokolski2013-232) Presently, Pakistan operates a [multi-party](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"),[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeBreenAllison-Reumann2023-233) with distinct [checks and balances](/wiki/Check_and_balance "Check and balance") among government branches.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloor2023-234) The first successful [democratic transition](/wiki/2013_Pakistani_general_election "2013 Pakistani general election") occurred in May 2013.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEB._Chakma2014-235) Pakistani politics revolves around a blend of [socialism](/wiki/Socialism_in_Pakistan "Socialism in Pakistan"), [conservatism](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Pakistan "Conservatism in Pakistan"), and the [third way](/wiki/Third_way "Third way"),[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChengappa2002-236) with the three main political parties being the conservative [Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"), socialist [Pakistani People's Party](/wiki/Pakistani_People%27s_Party "Pakistani People's Party"), and centrist [Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf](/wiki/Pakistan_Tehreek-e-Insaf "Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECRS2023-237) Constitutional amendments in 2010 curtailed presidential powers, enhancing the role of the prime minister.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERafiqAhmad2016-238)

* [Head of State](/wiki/Head_of_State "Head of State"): The ceremonial head of the state and civilian [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Pakistan Armed Forces](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces "Pakistan Armed Forces") is the [President](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan"), elected by an [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_of_Pakistan "Electoral College of Pakistan").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECRS2023-237) The [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Pakistan "Prime Minister of Pakistan") advises the President on key appointments, including military and judicial positions, and the President is constitutionally bound to act on this advice.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAziz2018-239)[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEF._Hussain2015-240) The President also holds powers to pardon and grant clemency.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahmood1965-241)
* [Legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative"): The [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") legislature includes a 96-member [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Pakistan "Senate of Pakistan") ([upper house](/wiki/Upper_House "Upper House")) and a 336-member [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "National Assembly of Pakistan") ([lower house](/wiki/Lower_House "Lower House")). [National Assembly members](/wiki/Member_of_the_National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "Member of the National Assembly of Pakistan") are elected via [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") under [universal adult suffrage](/wiki/Universal_adult_suffrage "Universal adult suffrage"), representing National Assembly [constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Pakistan "Constituencies of Pakistan"). The constitution reserves 70 seats for women and religious minorities, allocated to political parties based on proportional representation. Senate members are elected by provincial legislators, ensuring equal representation across all provinces.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYapAbeyratne2023272-242)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/A_night_side_view_of_Prime_Minister%27s_Secretariat_Building.jpg/250px-A_night_side_view_of_Prime_Minister%27s_Secretariat_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_night_side_view_of_Prime_Minister%27s_Secretariat_Building.jpg)

[Prime Minister's Office](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Office_(Pakistan) "Prime Minister's Office (Pakistan)")

* Executive: The Prime Minister, typically the leader of the [majority rule](/wiki/Majority_rule "Majority rule") party or coalition in the National Assembly (the lower house),[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDowdingDumont2014-243) serves as the country's chief executive and head of government. Responsibilities include forming a [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Pakistan "Cabinet of Pakistan"),[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZierkeStockmannMeyer2023-244) making executive decisions,[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAziz2018-239) and appointing senior civil servants, subject to executive confirmation.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEstablishment_Division2013-245)
* [Provincial governments](/wiki/List_of_provincial_governments_of_Pakistan "List of provincial governments of Pakistan"): Each of the [four provinces](/wiki/Four_Provinces_(Pakistan) "Four Provinces (Pakistan)") follows a similar governance system, with a directly elected [Provincial Assembly](/wiki/Category:Provincial_Assemblies_of_Pakistan "Category:Provincial Assemblies of Pakistan") choosing the [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_(Pakistan) "Chief Minister (Pakistan)"), usually from the largest party or coalition. Chief Ministers lead the provincial cabinet and oversee provincial governance.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahmood2007-246)[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIFES2013-247) The [Chief Secretary](/wiki/Chief_Secretary_(Pakistan) "Chief Secretary (Pakistan)"), appointed by the Prime Minister, heads the provincial bureaucracy.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEstablishment_Division2021-248) Provincial assemblies legislate and approve the provincial budget, typically presented by the provincial finance minister annually.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIFES2013-247)[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmadAsif2007-249) Ceremonial heads of provinces, the Provincial Governors, are appointed by the President based on the binding advice of the Prime Minister.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMahmood2007-246)[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESenate_of_Pakistan2018-250)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Supreme_Court_of_Pakistan%2C_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg/250px-Supreme_Court_of_Pakistan%2C_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg)](/wiki/File:Supreme_Court_of_Pakistan,_Islamabad_by_Usman_Ghani.jpg)

[Supreme Court of Pakistan](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Pakistan "Supreme Court of Pakistan")

* [Judicature](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Pakistan "Judiciary of Pakistan"): The judiciary in Pakistan has two classes: the superior and subordinate judiciary. The superior judiciary includes the [Supreme Court of Pakistan](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Pakistan "Supreme Court of Pakistan"),[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWuBandyopadhyayLee2021-251) [Federal Shariat Court](/wiki/Federal_Shariat_Court "Federal Shariat Court"), and five [high courts](/wiki/High_courts_of_Pakistan "High courts of Pakistan"),[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJha2016-252) with the Supreme Court at the top. It's responsible for safeguarding the constitution.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWuBandyopadhyayLee2021-251) Azad Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan have their own court systems.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberst2018-253)[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEjaz2022-254)

### Role of Islam

See also: [Islam in Pakistan](/wiki/Islam_in_Pakistan "Islam in Pakistan")

Pakistan, the only country established in the name of Islam,[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEsposito2003-255) had overwhelming support among Muslims, especially in provinces like the [United Provinces](/wiki/United_Provinces_(1937%E2%80%9350) "United Provinces (1937–50)"), where Muslims were a minority.[[247]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-1-256) This idea, articulated by the Muslim League, the [Islamic clergy](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama"), and Jinnah, envisioned an [Islamic state](/wiki/Islamic_state "Islamic state").[[248]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-2-257) Jinnah, closely associated with the *ulama*, was described upon his death by [Maulana Shabbir Ahmad Usmani](/wiki/Maulana_Shabbir_Ahmad_Usmani "Maulana Shabbir Ahmad Usmani") as the greatest Muslim after [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb"), aspiring to unite Muslims worldwide under Islam.[[249]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-3-258)

The Objectives Resolution of March 1949 marked the initial step towards this goal, affirming God as the sole sovereign.[[116]](#cite_note-Hussain-2008-2-124)[[250]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2010-1-259) Muslim League leader [Chaudhry Khaliquzzaman](/wiki/Chaudhry_Khaliquzzaman "Chaudhry Khaliquzzaman") asserted that Pakistan could only truly become an Islamic state after bringing all believers of Islam into a single political unit.[[251]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-4-260) Keith Callard observed that Pakistanis believed in the essential unity of purpose and outlook in the Muslim world, expecting similar views on religion and nationality from Muslims worldwide.[[252]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2010-2-261)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Eid_prayers_at_the_Badshahi_Mosque.JPG/330px-Eid_prayers_at_the_Badshahi_Mosque.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eid_prayers_at_the_Badshahi_Mosque.JPG)

[Eid prayers](/wiki/Eid_prayers "Eid prayers") at the [Badshahi Mosque](/wiki/Badshahi_Mosque "Badshahi Mosque") in Lahore

Pakistan's desire for a united Islamic bloc, called Islamistan, wasn't supported by other Muslim governments,[[253]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2010-3-262) though figures like the Grand Mufti of Palestine, [Al-Haj Amin al-Husseini](/wiki/Amin_al-Husseini "Amin al-Husseini"), and leaders of the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood") were drawn to the country. Pakistan's desire for an international organization of Muslim countries was fulfilled in the 1970s when the [Organization of Islamic Conference](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") (OIC) was formed.[[254]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2010-4-263) In East Pakistan, Bengali Muslims increasingly prioritised Bengali ethno-nationalism over religious nationalism.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENazrul_IslamSaidul_Islam2018-264) The Islamist party [Jamaat-e-Islami](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami "Jamaat-e-Islami") backed an Islamic state and opposed [Bengali nationalism](/wiki/Bengali_nationalism "Bengali nationalism").[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELintner2002-265)

After the 1970 general elections, the Parliament crafted the [1973 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan").[[257]](#cite_note-Diamantides-Gearey-2011-1-266) It declared Pakistan an Islamic Republic, with Islam as the state religion, and mandated laws to comply with Islamic teachings laid down in the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") and [Sunnah](/wiki/Sunnah "Sunnah") and that no law repugnant to such injunctions could be enacted.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIqbal2009-267) Additionally, it established institutions like the Shariat Court and the [Council of Islamic Ideology](/wiki/Council_of_Islamic_Ideology "Council of Islamic Ideology") to interpret and apply Islam.[[259]](#cite_note-Diamantides-Gearey-2011-2-268)

Zulfikar Ali Bhutto faced opposition under the banner of *Nizam-e-Mustafa* ("Rule of the [Prophet](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")"),[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENasr1996-269) advocating an Islamic state. Bhutto conceded to some Islamist demands before being ousted in a coup.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKepel2006-270)

General Zia-ul-Haq, after seizing power, committed to establishing an Islamic state and enforcing *[sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia")* law.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKepel2006-270) He instituted Shariat judicial courts,[[262]](#cite_note-Diamantides-Gearey-2011-3-271) and court benches,[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaqqani2010400-272)[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsia_Watch_Committee_(U.S.)1992-273) to adjudicate using Islamic doctrine.[[265]](#cite_note-Wynbrandt-2009-274) Zia aligned with Deobandi institutions,[[266]](#cite_note-Syed-Pio-Kamran-Zaidi-2016-1-275) exacerbating sectarian tensions with anti-Shia policies.[[267]](#cite_note-Syed-Pio-Kamran-Zaidi-2016-2-276)

Most Pakistanis, according to a [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") (PEW) poll, favor Sharia law as the official law,[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiegfeld2016-277) and 94 percent of them identify more with religion than nationality compared to Muslims in other nations.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPRC2011-278)
