Pakistan, a [federal](/wiki/Federal_republic "Federal republic") [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic"), consists of [four provinces](/wiki/Four_Provinces_(Pakistan) "Four Provinces (Pakistan)"): Punjab, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Sindh, and Balochistan, along with three [territories](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan"): [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"), [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan"), and [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir").[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdibelli_et_al.2022-286) The Government of Pakistan governs the [western parts](/wiki/Western_Kashmir "Western Kashmir") of the [Kashmir Region](/wiki/Kashmir_Region "Kashmir Region"), organized into separate political entities, Azad Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJan2015-287) In 2009, the [constitutional assignment](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan") (the *Gilgit–Baltistan Empowerment and Self-Governance Order*) granted Gilgit-Baltistan [semi-provincial status](/wiki/Provinces_of_Pakistan "Provinces of Pakistan"), providing it with self-government.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELansfordMuller2012-288)

The [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Pakistan "Local government in Pakistan") system consists of [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan"), [tehsils](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil"), and [union councils](/wiki/Union_Councils_of_Pakistan "Union Councils of Pakistan"), with an elected body at each tier.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBermanSabharwal2017-289)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Pakistan](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Pakistan "Foreign relations of Pakistan")

Since independence, Pakistan has aimed to maintain an independent foreign policy.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELodhi2022-290) Pakistan's [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_Pakistan "Foreign policy of Pakistan") and [geostrategy](/wiki/Pakistani_geostrategy "Pakistani geostrategy") focus on the economy, security, [national identity](/wiki/Nationalism_in_Pakistan "Nationalism in Pakistan"), and territorial integrity, as well as building close ties with other Muslim nations.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamid_et_al.2023-291) According to [Hasan Askari Rizvi](/wiki/Hasan_Askari_Rizvi "Hasan Askari Rizvi"), a foreign policy expert, "Pakistan highlights sovereign equality of states, bilateralism, mutuality of interests, and non-interference in each other's domestic affairs as the cardinal features of its foreign policy."[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERizvi2004-292)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/President_John_F._Kennedy_Meets_with_Mohammad_Ayub_Khan%2C_President_of_Pakistan_%2802%29.jpg/250px-President_John_F._Kennedy_Meets_with_Mohammad_Ayub_Khan%2C_President_of_Pakistan_%2802%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_John_F._Kennedy_Meets_with_Mohammad_Ayub_Khan,_President_of_Pakistan_(02).jpg)

President [Ayub Khan](/wiki/Ayub_Khan "Ayub Khan") meeting with U.S. President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") in 1961[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPicone2020-293)

The [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") remains a major issue between Pakistan and India, with three of their [four wars](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_wars_and_conflicts "Indo-Pakistani wars and conflicts") fought over it.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart-IngersollFrazier2012-294) Due partly to strained relations with India, Pakistan has close ties with Turkey and Iran, both focal points in its foreign policy.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnwar2006-295) Saudi Arabia also holds importance in Pakistan's foreign relations.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPande2011167-296)

As a non-signatory of the [Treaty on Nuclear Non-Proliferation](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons"), Pakistan holds influence in the [IAEA](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency").[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChakma2012-297) For years, Pakistan has blocked an [international treaty](/wiki/Fissile_Material_Cut-off_Treaty "Fissile Material Cut-off Treaty") to limit [fissile material](/wiki/Fissile_material "Fissile material"), arguing that its stockpile does not meet its long-term needs.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKmentt2021-298) Pakistan's nuclear program in the 20th century aimed to counter India's nuclear ambitions in the [region](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), and [reciprocal nuclear tests](/wiki/Pakistan%27s_nuclear_testing_series "Pakistan's nuclear testing series") ensued after India's [nuclear tests](/wiki/Pokhran-II "Pokhran-II"), solidifying Pakistan as a [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Pakistan "Nuclear power in Pakistan").[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIzuyamaOgawa2003-299) Pakistan maintains a policy of [Full spectrum deterrence](/wiki/N-deterrence "N-deterrence"), considering its nuclear program vital for [deterring](/wiki/Pakistan_and_its_Nuclear_Deterrent_Program "Pakistan and its Nuclear Deterrent Program") foreign aggression.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoor2023-300)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/SCO_meeting_%282022-09-16%29.jpg/250px-SCO_meeting_%282022-09-16%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SCO_meeting_(2022-09-16).jpg)

Prime Minister [Shehbaz Sharif](/wiki/Shehbaz_Sharif "Shehbaz Sharif") at the 2022 [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation") summit[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmbassy_of_the_Russian_Federation_to_the_Republic_of_Malta2022-301)

Located strategically in the world's major maritime oil supply lines and communication [fiber optic corridors](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber"), Pakistan also enjoys proximity to the natural resources of Central Asian countries.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShah1997-302) Pakistan actively participates in the United Nations with a [Permanent Representative](/wiki/Permanent_Representative_of_Pakistan_to_the_United_Nations "Permanent Representative of Pakistan to the United Nations") representing its positions in international politics.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasi2005-303) It has advocated for the concept of "[enlightened moderation](/wiki/Enlightened_moderation "Enlightened moderation")" in the Muslim world.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZahraBouckaertJadoonJabeen2022-304) Pakistan is a member of the Commonwealth of Nations, [SAARC](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation"), [ECO](/wiki/Economic_Cooperation_Organization "Economic Cooperation Organization"),[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2016-305)[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemal2004-306) and the [G20 developing nations](/wiki/G20_developing_nations "G20 developing nations").[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoekmanKostecki2009-307)

Pakistan is designated as an "Iron Brother" by China, emphasizing the significance of their close and supportive [relationship](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Pakistan_relations "China–Pakistan relations").[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQingyan2021-308) In the 1950s, Pakistan opposed the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") for geopolitical reasons. During the [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") in the 1980s, it was a close ally of the United States.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERizvi2004-292) Relations with Russia have improved since the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"),[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClary2022-309) but Pakistan's relationship with the United States has been "on-and-off".[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERizvi2004-292) Initially a close ally during the Cold War,[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKarat2007-310) Pakistan's relations with the US soured in the 1990s due to [sanctions](/wiki/Pressler_amendment "Pressler amendment") over its secretive nuclear program.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMazzetti2013-311) Since [9/11](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), Pakistan has been a US ally on [counterterrorism](/wiki/Counterterrorism "Counterterrorism"), but their relationship has been strained due to diverging interests and mistrust during the [20-year war](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") and terrorism issues. Although Pakistan was granted [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") status by the U.S. in 2004,[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaidiAhmad2021-312) it faced accusations of supporting the [Taliban insurgents](/wiki/Taliban_insurgency "Taliban insurgency") in Afghanistan.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYousafzai2021-313)

Pakistan does not have formal [diplomatic relations](/wiki/Pakistan-Israel_relations "Pakistan-Israel relations") with [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"); nonetheless, an exchange occurred between the two countries in 2005, with Turkey acting as an intermediary.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZelnick2013-314)

#### Relations with China

Main article: [China–Pakistan relations](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Pakistan_relations "China–Pakistan relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Huseyn_Shaheed_Suhrawardy_and_Zhou_Enlai_signing_the_Treaty_of_Friendship_Between_China_and_Pakistan_in_Beijing.jpg/250px-Huseyn_Shaheed_Suhrawardy_and_Zhou_Enlai_signing_the_Treaty_of_Friendship_Between_China_and_Pakistan_in_Beijing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huseyn_Shaheed_Suhrawardy_and_Zhou_Enlai_signing_the_Treaty_of_Friendship_Between_China_and_Pakistan_in_Beijing.jpg)

Pakistani Prime Minister [Huseyn Shaheed Suhrawardy](/wiki/Huseyn_Shaheed_Suhrawardy "Huseyn Shaheed Suhrawardy") with Chinese Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") signing the Treaty of Friendship Between China and Pakistan.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Tonder2018-315) Pakistan is host to China's largest embassy.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchuurmans202373-316)

Pakistan was among the first nations to establish formal diplomatic ties with China,[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen2011-317) forging a strong relationship since China's [1962 conflict](/wiki/Sino-Indian_War "Sino-Indian War") with India, culminating in a special bond.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwinghammer2018-318) During the 1970s, Pakistan acted as an intermediary in U.S.-China rapprochement,[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAfridiBajoria2010-319) facilitating US President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon")'s [historic visit](/wiki/1972_Nixon_visit_to_China "1972 Nixon visit to China") to China.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoos2024-320)[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELordMastroNaftaliBrinkley2022-321) Despite changes in Pakistani governance and regional/global dynamics, China's influence in Pakistan remains paramount.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAfridiBajoria2010-319) In reciprocation, China stands as Pakistan's largest trading partner, with substantial investment in Pakistani infrastructure, notably the [Gwadar port](/wiki/Gwadar_port "Gwadar port").[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaju2021-322) In 2015 alone, they inked 51 agreements and Memorandums of Understanding (MoUs) for cooperative efforts.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERimmer2020-323) Both nations signed a [Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Pakistan_Free_Trade_Agreement "China–Pakistan Free Trade Agreement") in 2006,[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZreik2024-324) with China making its largest investment in Pakistan's history through [CPEC](/wiki/CPEC "CPEC").[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorsey2018-325) Pakistan acts as China's liaison to the Muslim world,[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShih2022-326) and both nations support each other on sensitive issues like Kashmir, Taiwan, Xinjiang, and more.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPant2011-327)

#### Relations with the Muslim world

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/OIC_Leaders_in_Shalimar_Gardens%2C_Lahore.jpg/250px-OIC_Leaders_in_Shalimar_Gardens%2C_Lahore.jpg)](/wiki/File:OIC_Leaders_in_Shalimar_Gardens,_Lahore.jpg)

Pakistani Prime Minister [Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto") with leaders from [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), February 1974. That year, Pakistan hosted 36 Muslim countries at the [Second Islamic Summit](/wiki/Islamic_Summit_of_the_Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation#List_of_summits "Islamic Summit of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation").

After Independence, Pakistan vigorously pursued bilateral relations with other Muslim countries.[[319]](#cite_note-Pasha-2005-1-328) The [Ali](/wiki/Maulana_Muhammad_Ali_Jauhar "Maulana Muhammad Ali Jauhar") brothers sought to project Pakistan as the natural leader of the Islamic world, partly due to its significant manpower and military strength.[[320]](#cite_note-Pasha-2005-2-329) [Khaliquzzaman](/wiki/Chaudhry_Khaliquzzaman "Chaudhry Khaliquzzaman"), a prominent [Muslim League](/wiki/All_India_Muslim_League "All India Muslim League") leader, declared Pakistan's ambition to unite all Muslim countries into [Islamistan](/wiki/Islamistan "Islamistan"), a pan-Islamic entity.[[321]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-5-330)

These developments, alongside Pakistan's creation, didn't receive approval from the United States, with British Prime Minister [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee") expressing a hope for India and Pakistan to reunite.[[322]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2013-1-331) However, due to a nationalist awakening in the Arab world at that time, there was little interest in Pakistan's Pan-Islamic aspirations.[[323]](#cite_note-Haqqani-2013-2-332) Some Arab countries perceived the 'Islamistan' project as Pakistan's bid to dominate other Muslim states.[[324]](#cite_note-Roberts-2003-333)

Pakistan's founder, [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah"), consistently advocated for the [Palestinian cause](/wiki/Palestinian_nationalism "Palestinian nationalism"), shaping Pakistan's foreign policy to support Palestinian rights within the broader framework of Muslim solidarity.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJafriSultanaIjaz2021-334) During the [1967 Arab-Israel war](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), Pakistan supported the Arab states and played a key role in securing Iran's backing for the Arab cause both within the U.N. and beyond.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAroraGrover1995-335)

Pakistan's relations with Iran have been strained by sectarian tensions,[[327]](#cite_note-Hunter-2010-336) with both Iran and Saudi Arabia using Pakistan as a battleground for their proxy sectarian war.[[328]](#cite_note-Pande-2011-2-337) Since the early days of the [Iran–Iraq war](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_war "Iran–Iraq war"), President [Zia-ul-Haq](/wiki/Zia-ul-Haq "Zia-ul-Haq") played an important mediatory role, with Pakistan actively engaging in efforts to end the conflict.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETalbot2020-338)[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoseHusain1985-339) Pakistan provided support to Saudi Arabia during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War").[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalladayMateiBruneau2021-340) Pakistan chose to remain neutral during [Operation Decisive Storm](/wiki/Operation_Decisive_Storm "Operation Decisive Storm"), refraining from sending military support to Saudi Arabia in its offensive against [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"). Instead, Pakistan aimed to play a proactive diplomatic role in resolving the crisis,[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPanda2019-341) which led to tensions between the two countries.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalladayMateiBruneau2021-340) In 2016, Pakistan mediated between Saudi Arabia and Iran following the execution of Shia cleric [Nimr al-Nimr](/wiki/Nimr_al-Nimr "Nimr al-Nimr"), with visits to both countries by then Prime Minister [Nawaz Sharif](/wiki/Nawaz_Sharif "Nawaz Sharif") and the Chief of Army Staff, [Raheel Sharif](/wiki/Raheel_Sharif "Raheel Sharif").[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasharat2023-342)

Pakistan provided refuge to millions of displaced Afghans after the [Soviet invasion](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") and supported the [Afghan mujahideen](/wiki/Afghan_mujahideen "Afghan mujahideen") in their efforts to expel Soviet forces from Afghanistan.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiedel2010-343) After the Soviets withdrew, [infighting erupted among Mujahideen factions](/wiki/Afghan_Civil_War_(1989%E2%80%931992) "Afghan Civil War (1989–1992)") over control of Afghanistan. Pakistan facilitated [peace talks](/wiki/Peshawar_Accord "Peshawar Accord") to help end the conflict.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisokaRichmond2022-344) After four years of unresolved conflict between rival Mujahideen groups, Pakistan helped establish the Taliban as a stabilizing force.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaizland2023-345) Pakistan's support for the Sunni Taliban in Afghanistan challenged Shia-led Iran, which opposed a Taliban-controlled Afghanistan.[[328]](#cite_note-Pande-2011-2-337)

Pakistan vigorously advocated for self-determination among Muslims globally. Its efforts in supporting independence movements in countries like Indonesia, Algeria, Tunisia, Morocco, and [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") fostered strong ties.[[337]](#cite_note-Pande-2011-1-346) Due to its support for [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") in the [Nagorno-Karabakh conflict](/wiki/Nagorno-Karabakh_conflict "Nagorno-Karabakh conflict"), Pakistan has not established [diplomatic relations](/wiki/Armenia%E2%80%93Pakistan_relations "Armenia–Pakistan relations") with [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia").[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBergKursani2021-347)[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabayevSchochSpanger2019-348)

Pakistan and Bangladesh have experienced strained relations, particularly under the [Awami League](/wiki/Awami_League "Awami League") governments led by [Sheikh Hasina](/wiki/Sheikh_Hasina "Sheikh Hasina"), driven by her pro-India stance and historical grievances.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmedZahoor2019-349)

Pakistan, a prominent member of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") (OIC), prioritizes maintaining cultural, political, social, and economic relations with Arab and other Muslim-majority nations in its foreign policy.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPande2011-350)

In September 2025, Pakistan and Saudi Arabia signed a [Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement](/wiki/Strategic_Mutual_Defence_Agreement "Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement") under which any aggression against one country is considered an aggression against both, formalising a defensive commitment that also extends Pakistan's nuclear umbrella to Saudi Arabia.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKahwaji2025-351)

### Kashmir conflict

Main article: [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Kashmir_map.jpg/250px-Kashmir_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kashmir_map.jpg)

The areas shown in green are the Pakistani-controlled areas.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA1988-352)

[Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), a Himalayan region at the northern tip of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), was governed as the autonomous [princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)") during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") before the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in August 1947. This sparked a [major territorial dispute](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") between India and Pakistan, resulting in [several conflicts](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_wars_and_conflicts "Indo-Pakistani wars and conflicts") over the region. India controls about 45.1% of Kashmir, including [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") and [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh"), while Pakistan controls roughly 38.2%, comprising [Azad Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and [Gilgit−Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan"). Additionally, about 20% of the region, known as [Aksai Chin](/wiki/Aksai_Chin "Aksai Chin") and the [Shaksgam Valley](/wiki/Trans-Karakoram_Tract "Trans-Karakoram Tract"), is under Chinese control.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKudaisyaYong2004-353) India claims the entire Kashmir region based on the [Instrument of Accession](/wiki/Instrument_of_Accession_(Jammu_and_Kashmir) "Instrument of Accession (Jammu and Kashmir)") signed by the princely state's ruler, Maharaja Hari Singh,[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZahoorRumi2020-354) while Pakistan argues for its Muslim-majority population,[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhattacharyya2023-355) and geographical proximity to Pakistan.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaghavan2012-356) The United Nations was involved in resolving the conflict, leading to a ceasefire in 1949 and the establishment of the [Line of Control](/wiki/Line_of_Control "Line of Control") (LoC) as a *de facto* border.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone2017-357) India, fearing Kashmir's secession, did not hold the promised plebiscite, as it believed Kashmiris would vote to join Pakistan.[[349]](#cite_note-Endrst-1965-358)

Pakistan claims that its position is for the right of the [Kashmiri people](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris") to determine their future through impartial elections as mandated by the United Nations, while India has stated that Kashmir is an "[integral part](/wiki/Akhand_Bharat "Akhand Bharat")" of India, referring to the [1972 Simla Agreement](/wiki/Simla_Agreement "Simla Agreement") and to the fact that [regional elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Jammu_and_Kashmir "Elections in Jammu and Kashmir") take place regularly.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhurshid2016-359) Certain Kashmiri independence groups believe that Kashmir should be independent of both India and Pakistan.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOldenburg2019-360)

### Military

Main article: [Pakistan Armed Forces](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces "Pakistan Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Pakistan_Air_Force_JF-17_Thunder_flies_in_front_of_the_26%2C660_ft_high_Nanga_Parbat.jpg/250px-Pakistan_Air_Force_JF-17_Thunder_flies_in_front_of_the_26%2C660_ft_high_Nanga_Parbat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pakistan_Air_Force_JF-17_Thunder_flies_in_front_of_the_26,660_ft_high_Nanga_Parbat.jpg)

[Pakistan Air Force](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force")'s [JF-17 Thunder](/wiki/JF-17_Thunder "JF-17 Thunder") flying in front of the 8,130-metre-high (26,660-foot) [Nanga Parbat](/wiki/Nanga_Parbat "Nanga Parbat")

Pakistan is considered a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") nation,[[352]](#cite_note-Middle_power_nation-361)[[j]](#cite_note-RSCT-362) with the world's [seventh-largest standing armed forces](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel") in terms of personnel size, comprising approximately 660,000 active-duty troops and 291,000 paramilitary personnel as of 2024.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIISS2024-363) [Established in 1947](/wiki/Military_history_of_Pakistan "Military history of Pakistan"), the [armed forces of Pakistan](/wiki/Armed_forces_of_Pakistan "Armed forces of Pakistan") wielded significant influence over [national politics](/wiki/Politics_of_Pakistan "Politics of Pakistan").[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartholomees2008-364) The main branches include the [Army](/wiki/Pakistan_Army "Pakistan Army"), [Navy](/wiki/Pakistan_Navy "Pakistan Navy"), and [Air Force](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force"), supported by numerous [paramilitaries](/wiki/Civil_Armed_Forces "Civil Armed Forces").[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeRouenHeo2005-365)

The [Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Committee](/wiki/Chairman_Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff_Committee "Chairman Joint Chiefs of Staff Committee") (CJCSC) is the highest-ranking military officer, advising the civilian government. However, they lack direct command over the branches and serve as intermediaries, ensuring communication between the military and civilian leadership. Overseeing the [Joint Staff Headquarters](/wiki/Joint_Staff_Headquarters_(Pakistan) "Joint Staff Headquarters (Pakistan)"), they coordinate inter-service cooperation and joint military missions.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood1996287-366)

[Command and control](/wiki/Nuclear_command_and_control "Nuclear command and control") over Pakistan's [strategic arsenal](/wiki/Pakistan%27s_nuclear_testing_series "Pakistan's nuclear testing series") development and employment is vested in the [National Command Authority](/wiki/National_Command_Authority_(Pakistan) "National Command Authority (Pakistan)"), overseeing work on [nuclear doctrine](/wiki/Nuclear_Doctrine_of_Pakistan "Nuclear Doctrine of Pakistan") to maintain [Full spectrum deterrence](/wiki/Full_spectrum_deterrence "Full spectrum deterrence").[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2012-151)

The United States, Turkey, and China maintain close military relations with [Pakistan Armed Forces](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces "Pakistan Armed Forces"), regularly exporting military equipment and [technology transfer](/wiki/Technology_transfer "Technology transfer").[[357]](#cite_note-Military_relations-367) Pakistan was the 5th-largest recipient and importer of arms between 2019 and 2023.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWezeman_et_al.2024-368)

#### Military history

Main article: [Military history of Pakistan](/wiki/Military_history_of_Pakistan "Military history of Pakistan")

Since 1947, Pakistan has been involved in [four](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_wars_and_conflicts "Indo-Pakistani wars and conflicts") [conventional wars](/wiki/Conventional_warfare "Conventional warfare") with India.[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGatesRoy2016-369) The [first conflict](/wiki/Indo-Pak_war_of_1947 "Indo-Pak war of 1947") took place in Kashmir and ended in a United Nations-mediated ceasefire, with Pakistan gaining control of one-third of the region.[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOetzelTing-Toomey2006-370) Territorial disputes led to [another war in 1965](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1965 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1965"). In 1971, India and Pakistan fought [another war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971") over [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan"), with Indian forces aiding its independence, leading to the creation of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECenter_for_Preventive_Action2024-371) Tensions in Kargil brought the two countries to the [brink of war](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1999 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1999").[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusch2014-372)

Pakistan's primary intelligence agency, the Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI), was established within a year of Pakistan's independence in 1947.[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESprague2020-373) During the [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"), Pakistan's intelligence community, mostly the [ISI](/wiki/Inter-Services_Intelligence "Inter-Services Intelligence"), [coordinated](/wiki/Operation_Cyclone "Operation Cyclone") [US resources](/wiki/United_States_involvement_in_regime_change "United States involvement in regime change") to support [Afghan mujahideen](/wiki/Afghan_mujahideen "Afghan mujahideen") and [foreign fighters](/wiki/Afghan_Arabs "Afghan Arabs") against Soviet presence.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERupert1989-374) The [PAF](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force") engaged with [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Air_Forces "Soviet Air Forces") and [Afghan Air Forces](/wiki/Afghan_Air_Force "Afghan Air Force") during the [conflict](/wiki/Russian_war_in_afghanistan "Russian war in afghanistan").[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWithington2005-375) Pakistan has been an active [participant in UN peacekeeping missions](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping_missions_involving_Pakistan "United Nations peacekeeping missions involving Pakistan"),[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_ConingAoiKarlsrud2017-376) playing a major role in operations like the rescue mission in [Mogadishu](/wiki/Mogadishu "Mogadishu"), Somalia, in 1993.[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart2002-377) According to a 2023 UN report, the Pakistani military was the fifth largest troop contributor to UN peacekeeping missions.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUN2023-378)

Pakistan has [deployed its military](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces_deployments "Pakistan Armed Forces deployments") in some Arab countries, providing defense, training, and advisory roles.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECordesman1987-379)[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChengappa2004-380) The [PAF](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force")'s fighter pilots participated in missions against Israel during the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") and the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War").[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaruqui2019-381) Pakistani [special forces](/wiki/Special_Service_Group "Special Service Group") assisted [Saudi forces](/wiki/Saudi_forces "Saudi forces") in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca") during the [Grand Mosque Seizure](/wiki/Grand_Mosque_Seizure "Grand Mosque Seizure").[[372]](#cite_note-Miller-2015-382) Pakistan also sent 5,000 troops as part of a US-led coalition for the defense of Saudi Arabia during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War").[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERizvi1993-383)

Despite the UN arms embargo on [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia "Bosnia"), the ISI under General [Javed Nasir](/wiki/Javed_Nasir "Javed Nasir") airlifted anti-tank weapons and missiles to Bosnian mujahideen, shifting the tide in favor of Bosnian Muslims. ISI, under Nasir's leadership, supported Chinese Muslims in [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), rebel groups in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), and religious groups in Central Asia.[[374]](#cite_note-Wiebes-2003-384)[[375]](#cite_note-Abbas-2015-385)

Since 2001, the Pakistan military has been engaged in [counterinsurgency and internal security operations](/wiki/War_in_North-West_Pakistan "War in North-West Pakistan") in the [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") province, primarily targeting [Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan](/wiki/Tehrik-e-Taliban_Pakistan "Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan") and associated militant groups. Major military operations conducted during this period include [Operation Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom"), [Operation al-Mizan](/wiki/Operation_al-Mizan "Operation al-Mizan"), [Operation Zalzala](/wiki/Operation_Zalzala "Operation Zalzala"), [Operation Sherdil](/wiki/Operation_Sherdil "Operation Sherdil"), [Operation Rah-e-Haq](/wiki/Operation_Rah-e-Haq "Operation Rah-e-Haq"), [Operation Rah-e-Rast](/wiki/Operation_Rah-e-Rast "Operation Rah-e-Rast"), and [Operation Rah-e-Nijat](/wiki/Operation_Rah-e-Nijat "Operation Rah-e-Nijat").[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZA_Khan2012-386)

### Law enforcement

Main article: [Law enforcement in Pakistan](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Pakistan "Law enforcement in Pakistan")

See also: [Pakistani Intelligence Community](/wiki/Pakistani_Intelligence_Community "Pakistani Intelligence Community")

[Law enforcement in Pakistan](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Pakistan "Law enforcement in Pakistan") consists of federal and provincial police agencies. Each of the [four provinces](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") ([Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), and [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan")) has its own police force, while the [Islamabad Capital Territory (ICT)](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory") has the Islamabad Police.[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaishankar2019-387) Provincial police forces are led by an Inspector-General of Police (IGP), who is appointed from the federally recruited and trained [Police Service of Pakistan](/wiki/Police_Service_of_Pakistan "Police Service of Pakistan") (PSP) through a consultative process between the federal and provincial governments. All positions above the Assistant Superintendent level are filled from the PSP, ensuring national standards across provincial forces.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKureshiWaseem2024-388)

**Specialized Units:**

* [National Highways & Motorway Police](/wiki/National_Highways_%26_Motorway_Police "National Highways & Motorway Police") (NHMP): Enforces traffic laws and ensures safety on Pakistan's inter-provincial motorway network.[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEImamFatima2021-389)
* Specialized Rapid Response Units: Specialized counter-terrorism units, such as the [Punjab Elite Police Force](/wiki/Elite_Police "Elite Police"), have been trained by army commandos and exist in every province to respond to hostage situations and neutralize armed groups.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeritoParvez2024-390)

The [Civil Armed Forces](/wiki/Civil_Armed_Forces "Civil Armed Forces") (CAF) assist local law enforcement agencies and participate in border security and internal security operations, particularly in conflict-affected regions.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf2019-391)

In 2021, the [National Intelligence Coordination Committee](/wiki/National_Intelligence_Coordination_Committee_(Pakistan) "National Intelligence Coordination Committee (Pakistan)") was established to improve coordination among Pakistan's intelligence agencies. The inaugural meeting was attended by the heads of the [ISI](/wiki/Inter-Services_Intelligence "Inter-Services Intelligence"), [IB](/wiki/Intelligence_Bureau_(Pakistan) "Intelligence Bureau (Pakistan)"), and [FIA](/wiki/Federal_Investigation_Agency "Federal Investigation Agency").[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaeed2021-392)

### Human rights

Main articles: [Human rights in Pakistan](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Pakistan "Human rights in Pakistan") and [LGBT rights in Pakistan](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Pakistan "LGBT rights in Pakistan")

Pakistan scored 27 out of 100 on the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") in 2024, ranking 135th out of 180 countries in the assessment of perceived public-sector corruption.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECPI2025-393) In 2025, Pakistan ranked 158 out of 180 countries in the [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") by [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders"), highlighting restrictions on [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press").[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarès2025-394) According to the journalist [Raza Rumi](/wiki/Raza_Rumi "Raza Rumi"), in 2023, TV channels in Pakistan faced suspensions and legal threats for airing content critical of the government or military, while online platforms also experienced temporary takedowns.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKumar2023-395) According to a 2025 report, some newspapers have faced financial pressure—such as withdrawal of government advertisements—for publishing content critical of government policies. Both military and civilian governments have historically used such tactics.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGabolAli2025-396)

In Pakistan, all sexual activity outside of marriage is illegal.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerber2021-397) The punishment for sex outside marriage (*[zina](/wiki/Zina "Zina")*) ranges from up to five years' imprisonment for minors to 100 lashes for unmarried adults and [stoning to death](/wiki/Stoning_to_death "Stoning to death") for married adults, depending on marital status, age, sanity, and whether strict evidentiary requirements for a *[hadd](/wiki/Hadd "Hadd")* punishment—such as four adult male Muslim witnesses or a confession—are met; however, no one has been stoned to death under the law to date.[[388]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaderin2017-398) Male [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") is illegal in Pakistan and is punishable by up to ten years in prison, [corporal punishment](/wiki/Corporal_punishment "Corporal punishment") such as [whipping](/wiki/Whipping "Whipping"), and, under [Islamic law](/wiki/Islamic_law "Islamic law") since 1990, potentially even stoning.[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETin2008-399)

[Honour killings](/wiki/Honour_killings "Honour killings"), locally referred to as *[karo-kari](/wiki/Karo-kari "Karo-kari")* in parts of Pakistan, involve the killing of women and, less frequently, men by family or community members over alleged extramarital relationships or perceived violations of honour. Human rights organisations report that hundreds of women are killed each year, with many cases unreported.[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESingh_et_al.2024-400) Pakistan also has a high number of reported [acid attacks](/wiki/Acid_attacks "Acid attacks"), with about 200 incidents annually as of 2023, most victims being women. According to [Masarrat Misbah](/wiki/Masarrat_Misbah "Masarrat Misbah"), founder of the [Depilex Smileagain Foundation](/wiki/Depilex_Smileagain_Foundation "Depilex Smileagain Foundation"), women have been targeted for rejecting marriage proposals, giving birth to daughters instead of sons, or insufficient dowry. The 2011 Acid and Burn Crime Bill criminalised acid attacks, making them punishable by lengthy imprisonment and fines.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMubasharMir2023-401)

In parts of Pakistan, girls are given in marriage to settle disputes or crimes between families in a customary practice called *[Vani](/wiki/Vani_(custom) "Vani (custom)")*. Girls married through this practice are often treated as compensation for a dispute, which places them in a vulnerable social position within the family they marry into. In 2021, the [Federal Shariat Court](/wiki/Federal_Shariat_Court "Federal Shariat Court") declared the practice un-Islamic and unconstitutional, reaffirming earlier Supreme Court jurisprudence.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAftab2022-402)

[UN Women](/wiki/UN_Women "UN Women") reported in 2024 that around one in six girls in Pakistan are married during childhood, with the country home to nearly 19 million child brides.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUN_Women2024-403) In 2025, Pakistan enacted a law setting the minimum legal age for marriage at 18 and introducing penalties for arranging underage marriages; the law is limited to the [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory") and does not apply nationwide.[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalk_Free2025-404)

Pakistan's economy [ranks 27th](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") globally by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP) and [42nd by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)").[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(PPP)2025-405)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(nom)2025-407) Historically, Pakistan was part of the wealthiest [region](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") in the first millennium CE, but lost ground to regions like China and Western Europe by the 18th century.[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaddison2006-415) Pakistan is a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country"),[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPasillasBrundinMarkowska2017-416) and part of the [Next Eleven](/wiki/Next_Eleven "Next Eleven"), poised to become one of the world's largest economies in the 21st century, alongside the [BRIC](/wiki/BRIC_(economics_term) "BRIC (economics term)") countries.[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEComimAnandFennell2021-417) The semi-industrialised economy is heavily dependent on agriculture, and industrial growth in Pakistan benefits significantly from agricultural expansion.[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenneberryKhanPiewthongngam2000-418) In 2023, Pakistan was the 66th-largest export economy,[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOEC2023-419) with a trade deficit of US$21.3 billion.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank_(WITS)2025-420)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/KHIURBANSKYLINE.jpg/330px-KHIURBANSKYLINE.jpg)](/wiki/File:KHIURBANSKYLINE.jpg)

Rising skyline of [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), with several under construction skyscrapers

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pakistan&action=edit), Pakistan's [nominal GDP](/wiki/Nominal_GDP "Nominal GDP") stood at US$410.5 billion,[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(nom)2025-407) with GDP by [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") at US$1.67 trillion.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(PPP)2025-405) The nominal per capita GDP was US$1,710,[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(nom_per_capita)2025-408) and the [GDP (PPP)/capita](/wiki/GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "GDP (PPP) per capita") amounted to US$6,950 ([international dollars](/wiki/International_dollar "International dollar")).[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(PPP_per_capita)2025-406) Around 44.7% of the population live below the international poverty line of US$4.20 a day, 16.5% live in extreme poverty on less than US$3 a day,[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIqbal2025-421) 8% of the total labour force is unemployed, and [CPI](/wiki/Consumer_price_index "Consumer price index") inflation in 2025 was 4.5%.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIMF_(datasets)2025-409) Pakistan ranks near the bottom globally on the [Global Gender Gap Index](/wiki/Global_Gender_Gap_Index "Global Gender Gap Index")'s economic participation and opportunity sub-index for women, reflecting very low female labour force participation and limited representation of women in senior economic roles.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWEF2025-422) Pakistan's middle class is estimated to be around 40–50 million people, representing 15–20 per cent of the population.[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmad2024-423) Among [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") countries, Pakistan's GDP growth rate of 2.5% in 2024 was the second lowest after Afghanistan.[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEADB2025-424)

Pakistan has strong potential to produce diverse food commodities due to its vast natural resource base across varied ecological and climatic zones.[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharifJavedAbbasHassan2008-425) It has the world's 7th-largest [labour force](/wiki/Labour_in_Pakistan "Labour in Pakistan"),[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConte2025-426) and its [diaspora](/wiki/Pakistani_diaspora "Pakistani diaspora") of around 10 million,[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbas2026-427) sent US$38.3 billion in remittances in fiscal year 2024–25, as reported by the [Special Investment Facilitation Council](/wiki/Special_Investment_Facilitation_Council "Special Investment Facilitation Council").[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESIFC2025-428) Pakistan's share of global exports averaged 0.14% between 1990 and 2023 and was 0.12% in 2023.[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Global_Economy2023-429)

### Agriculture and mining sector

Main articles: [Agriculture in Pakistan](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Pakistan "Agriculture in Pakistan"), [Fuel extraction in Pakistan](/wiki/Fuel_extraction_in_Pakistan "Fuel extraction in Pakistan"), and [Mining in Pakistan](/wiki/Mining_in_Pakistan "Mining in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Pakistan_Chrome_Mines20120126_16100237_0003.jpg/250px-Pakistan_Chrome_Mines20120126_16100237_0003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pakistan_Chrome_Mines20120126_16100237_0003.jpg)

[Surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining") in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"). Pakistan has been termed the 'Saudi Arabia of Coal' by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*.[[420]](#cite_note-430)

The Pakistani economy has shifted from [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Pakistan "Agriculture in Pakistan") to services, with agriculture contributing only 20.9% of the GDP as of 2015.[[421]](#cite_note-DSEC-431) Despite this, Pakistan's wheat production in 2005 surpassed Africa's and nearly matched South America's, highlighting its agricultural significance.[[422]](#cite_note-432) The sector employs 43.5% of the labor force and is a major source of foreign exchange.[[421]](#cite_note-DSEC-431)[[423]](#cite_note-433)

Manufactured exports, heavily reliant on agricultural raw materials like cotton and hides, face inflationary pressures due to supply shortages and market disruptions. Pakistan ranks fifth in cotton production, self-sufficient in sugarcane, and the fourth-largest milk producer globally. Though land and water resources haven't increased proportionately, productivity gains, especially from the [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") in the late 1960s and 1970s, significantly boosted wheat and rice yields. Private tube wells and High Yielding Varieties (HYVs) further augmented crop yields.[[424]](#cite_note-434) Meat industry accounts for 1.4 percent of overall GDP.[[425]](#cite_note-435)

### Industry

Main article: [Industry of Pakistan](/wiki/Industry_of_Pakistan "Industry of Pakistan")

See also: [Textile industry in Pakistan](/wiki/Textile_industry_in_Pakistan "Textile industry in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Tv_Assembly_Line_1.jpg/250px-Tv_Assembly_Line_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tv_Assembly_Line_1.jpg)

Television assembly factory in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"). Pakistan's industrial sector accounts for about 20% of the GDP, and is dominated by [small and medium-sized enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium-sized_enterprises "Small and medium-sized enterprises").[[426]](#cite_note-436)

Industry, constituting 19.74% of GDP and 24% of total employment, is the second-largest sector. Large-scale manufacturing (LSM) dominates, representing 12.2% of GDP, with cement production thriving due to demand from Afghanistan and the domestic real estate sector.[[427]](#cite_note-437) In 2013, Pakistan exported 7,708,557 metric tons of cement, with an installed capacity of 44,768,250 metric tons.[[428]](#cite_note-438) The [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry_in_Pakistan "Textile industry in Pakistan"), a key player in Pakistan's manufacturing, contributes 9.5% to GDP and employs around 15 million people. As of 2022, Pakistan ranks seventh globally in cotton production,[[429]](#cite_note-439) with substantial spinning capacity, making it a major exporter of textile products in Asia.[[430]](#cite_note-440) China has been a significant buyer of Pakistani textiles, importing US$1.527 billion worth of textiles in 2012.[[431]](#cite_note-441)

### Services

As of 2014–15, the services sector contributes 58.8% to GDP,[[421]](#cite_note-DSEC-431) serving as the main driver of economic growth in Pakistan,[[432]](#cite_note-442) with a consumption-oriented society. The sector's growth rate surpasses that of agriculture and industry, accounting for 54% of GDP and over one-third of total employment. It has strong linkages with other sectors, providing essential inputs to agriculture and manufacturing.[[433]](#cite_note-443) Pakistan's IT sector is one of the fastest-growing, ranked 110th for ICT development by the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum").[[434]](#cite_note-444) With around 82 million internet users as of May 2020, Pakistan [ranks](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_Internet_users "List of countries by number of Internet users") among top ten globally,[[435]](#cite_note-PTD-445) and its ICT industry is projected to exceed $10 billion by 2020.[[436]](#cite_note-446) With 12,000 employees, Pakistan is among the top five freelancing nations,[[437]](#cite_note-447) and its export performance in telecom, computer, and information services has notably improved.[[438]](#cite_note-448)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Pakistan](/wiki/Tourism_in_Pakistan "Tourism in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Shangrila%2C_Lower_Kachura_Lake.jpg/250px-Shangrila%2C_Lower_Kachura_Lake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shangrila,_Lower_Kachura_Lake.jpg)

[Shangrila Lake](/wiki/Shangrila_Lake "Shangrila Lake") and adjoining resort in [Skardu](/wiki/Skardu "Skardu"), [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan")

With its diverse cultures, landscapes, and attractions, Pakistan drew around 6.6 million foreign tourists in 2018.[[439]](#cite_note-449) However, this was a decline from the peak of tourism in the 1970s driven by the popular [Hippie trail](/wiki/Hippie_trail "Hippie trail").[[440]](#cite_note-450) Pakistan boasts attractions from [mangroves](/wiki/Indus_River_Delta-Arabian_Sea_mangroves "Indus River Delta-Arabian Sea mangroves") in the south to Himalayan [hill stations](/wiki/List_of_hill_stations_of_Pakistan "List of hill stations of Pakistan") in the northeast, including ancient Buddhist ruins of [Takht-i-Bahi](/wiki/Takht-i-Bahi "Takht-i-Bahi") and [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila"), the 5,000-year-old [Indus Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilization "Indus Valley civilization") sites such as [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro") and [Harappa](/wiki/Harappa "Harappa"),[[441]](#cite_note-451) and numerous [mountain peaks](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Pakistan "List of mountains in Pakistan") over 7,000 metres (23,000 feet).[[442]](#cite_note-452) The northern part of Pakistan boasts numerous old fortresses, showcasing ancient architecture. It encompasses the [Hunza](/wiki/Hunza_Valley "Hunza Valley") and [Chitral valleys](/wiki/Chitral_valley "Chitral valley"), where the small pre-Islamic [Kalasha](/wiki/Kalash_people "Kalash people") community resides, claiming descent from Alexander the Great.[[443]](#cite_note-453) Lahore, Pakistan's cultural capital, showcases numerous examples of [Mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture"), including the [Badshahi Masjid](/wiki/Badshahi_Masjid "Badshahi Masjid"), the [Shalimar Gardens](/wiki/Shalimar_Gardens_(Lahore) "Shalimar Gardens (Lahore)"), the [Tomb of Jahangir](/wiki/Tomb_of_Jahangir "Tomb of Jahangir"), and the [Lahore Fort](/wiki/Lahore_Fort "Lahore Fort"). Following the [2005 Kashmir earthquake](/wiki/2005_Kashmir_earthquake "2005 Kashmir earthquake"), *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* highlighted "The top five tourist sites in Pakistan" to boost tourism, featuring destinations like [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), the [Karakoram Highway](/wiki/Karakoram_Highway "Karakoram Highway"), [Karimabad](/wiki/Karimabad_(Hunza) "Karimabad (Hunza)"), and [Lake Saiful Muluk](/wiki/Lake_Saiful_Muluk "Lake Saiful Muluk").[[444]](#cite_note-454) Festivals and government initiatives aim to promote Pakistan's cultural heritage.[[445]](#cite_note-455) In 2015, the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") ranked Pakistan 125th out of 141 countries in its Travel & Tourism Competitiveness Report.[[446]](#cite_note-456)

## Infrastructure

See also: [Water supply and sanitation in Pakistan](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Pakistan "Water supply and sanitation in Pakistan")

Pakistan was lauded as the top nation for infrastructure development in South Asia during the 2016 annual meetings of the IMF and World Bank.[[447]](#cite_note-457)

### Power and energy

Main articles: [Nuclear power in Pakistan](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Pakistan "Nuclear power in Pakistan"), [Energy in Pakistan](/wiki/Energy_in_Pakistan "Energy in Pakistan"), and [Electricity sector in Pakistan](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Pakistan "Electricity sector in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Tarbela_Dam_during_the_2010_floods.jpg/250px-Tarbela_Dam_during_the_2010_floods.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tarbela_Dam_during_the_2010_floods.jpg)

[Tarbela Dam](/wiki/Tarbela_Dam "Tarbela Dam"), the largest earth filled dam in the world, was constructed in 1968.

As of May 2021, Pakistan operates six licensed commercial [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plants "Nuclear power plants").[[448]](#cite_note-458) The [Pakistan Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/Pakistan_Atomic_Energy_Commission "Pakistan Atomic Energy Commission") (PAEC) oversees these plants, while the [Pakistan Nuclear Regulatory Authority](/wiki/Pakistan_Nuclear_Regulatory_Authority "Pakistan Nuclear Regulatory Authority") ensures their safe operation.[[449]](#cite_note-459) These plants contribute approximately 5.8% to Pakistan's electricity supply, while fossil fuels (crude oil and natural gas) provide 64.2%, [hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectric_power "Hydroelectric power") provides 29.9%, and [coal](/wiki/Coal_power_plant "Coal power plant") contributes 0.1%.[[450]](#cite_note-Express_Tribune,_2014-460)[[451]](#cite_note-461) The [KANUPP-I](/wiki/Karachi_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Karachi Nuclear Power Plant"), Pakistan's first commercial nuclear power plant, was supplied by Canada in 1971. Sino-Pakistani nuclear cooperation began in the 1980s, leading to the establishment of CHASNUPP-I. In 2005, both countries proposed a joint energy security plan, aiming for a generation capacity exceeding 160,000 [MWe](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") by 2030. Pakistan's Nuclear Energy Vision 2050 targets a capacity of 40,000 MWe,[[452]](#cite_note-462) with 8,900 MWe expected by 2030.[[453]](#cite_note-463)

In June 2008, the nuclear complex at [Chashma](/wiki/Chashma_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chashma Nuclear Power Plant") in Punjab Province expanded with the installation of [Chashma-III](/wiki/Chashma_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chashma Nuclear Power Plant") and [Chashma–IV](/wiki/Chashma_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Chashma Nuclear Power Plant") reactors, each with 325–340 MWe, costing [₨](/wiki/Pakistani_rupee "Pakistani rupee")129 billion, with ₨80 billion from international sources, mainly China. Another agreement for China's assistance was signed in October 2008, seen as a response to the [US–India agreement](/wiki/India%E2%80%93United_States_Civil_Nuclear_Agreement "India–United States Civil Nuclear Agreement"). The project's cost was then US$1.7 billion, with a foreign loan of US$1.07 billion. In 2013, Pakistan established a second [nuclear complex](/wiki/Karachi_Nuclear_Power_Complex "Karachi Nuclear Power Complex") in Karachi with plans for additional reactors, similar to Chashma.[[454]](#cite_note-464) [Electrical energy in Pakistan](/wiki/Electricity_in_Pakistan "Electricity in Pakistan") is generated by [various corporations](/wiki/Electricity_distribution_companies_of_Pakistan "Electricity distribution companies of Pakistan") and distributed evenly among the [four provinces](/wiki/Four_Provinces_(Pakistan) "Four Provinces (Pakistan)") by the [National Electric Power Regulatory Authority](/wiki/National_Electric_Power_Regulatory_Authority "National Electric Power Regulatory Authority") (NEPRA). However, Karachi-based [K-Electric](/wiki/K-Electric "K-Electric") and [Water and Power Development Authority](/wiki/Water_and_Power_Development_Authority "Water and Power Development Authority") (WAPDA) generate much of the electricity used in Pakistan and collect revenue nationwide.[[455]](#cite_note-465) In 2023, Pakistan's installed [electricity generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") capacity was ~45,885 MWt.[[456]](#cite_note-466) Pakistan produced 1,135 megawatts of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") for the month of October 2016. Pakistan expects to produce 10,000 megawatts of renewable energy by 2025.[[457]](#cite_note-467)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Pakistan](/wiki/Transport_in_Pakistan "Transport in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/M2-Motorway.jpg/250px-M2-Motorway.jpg)](/wiki/File:M2-Motorway.jpg)

The motorway passes through the [Salt Range](/wiki/Salt_Range "Salt Range") mountains.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/PK_Karachi_asv2020-02_img54_Cantonment_Railway_Station.jpg/250px-PK_Karachi_asv2020-02_img54_Cantonment_Railway_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:PK_Karachi_asv2020-02_img54_Cantonment_Railway_Station.jpg)

[Karachi Cantonment railway station](/wiki/Karachi_Cantonment_railway_station "Karachi Cantonment railway station")

Pakistan boasts 2567 km of motorways and approximately 263,942 km of highways, which handle 92% of passengers and 96% of freight traffic. Despite constituting only 4.6% of the total road length, these north–south links manage 85% of the nation's traffic. They connect southern seaports such as [Karachi port](/wiki/Port_of_Karachi "Port of Karachi") and [Port Qasim](/wiki/Port_Qasim "Port Qasim") in Sindh, along with [Gwadar Port](/wiki/Gwadar_port "Gwadar port") and [Port of Pasni](/wiki/Port_of_Pasni "Port of Pasni") in Balochistan, to populous provinces like Punjab and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa domestically, and neighboring countries like Afghanistan, [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and China through the [China Pakistan Economic Corridor](/wiki/China_Pakistan_Economic_Corridor "China Pakistan Economic Corridor").[[458]](#cite_note-nation-468)[[459]](#cite_note-pc-469)[[460]](#cite_note-470)[[461]](#cite_note-471) According to the [WEF](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum")'s Global Competitiveness Report, Pakistan's port infrastructure quality ratings rose from 3.7 to 4.1 between 2007 and 2016.[[462]](#cite_note-472) The railway's share of inland traffic is reduced to below 8% for passengers and 4% for freight.[[421]](#cite_note-DSEC-431) This shift led to a decrease in total rail track from 8,775 kilometres (5,453 miles) in 1990–91 to 7,791 kilometres (4,841 miles) in 2011.[[459]](#cite_note-pc-469)[[458]](#cite_note-nation-468)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/KKH.png/250px-KKH.png)](/wiki/File:KKH.png)

[Karakoram Highway](/wiki/Karakoram_Highway "Karakoram Highway"), connecting Pakistan to China, is one of the highest paved roads in the world.

The transport landscape of Pakistan features various [modern transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") systems. The [Orange Line Metro Train](/wiki/Orange_Line_(Lahore_Metro) "Orange Line (Lahore Metro)") in Lahore, inaugurated in 2020,[[463]](#cite_note-473) spans 27.1 km (16.8 mi),[[464]](#cite_note-474) and includes both elevated and underground sections, accommodating over 250,000 passengers daily.[[465]](#cite_note-475) Lahore also boasts the [Lahore Metrobus](/wiki/Lahore_Metrobus "Lahore Metrobus"), the first of its kind in Pakistan, operational since February 2013.[[466]](#cite_note-476) The [Rawalpindi-Islamabad Metrobus](/wiki/Rawalpindi-Islamabad_Metrobus "Rawalpindi-Islamabad Metrobus"), stretching 48.1 km, commenced its first phase in June 2015, with subsequent extensions, and employs e-ticketing and an Intelligent Transportation System.[[467]](#cite_note-Orange_line_trial_run-477)[[468]](#cite_note-PM_Shehbaz_Sharif_inaugurates_metrobus_service-478) [Multan Metrobus](/wiki/Multan_Metrobus "Multan Metrobus"), inaugurated in January 2017, serves [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan") with its rapid transit services.[[469]](#cite_note-479)[[470]](#cite_note-480) [Peshawar's Bus Rapid Transit](/wiki/Peshawar_Metrobus "Peshawar Metrobus"), inaugurated in August 2020, marks the fourth BRT system in Pakistan. Karachi's [Green Line Metrobus](/wiki/Green_Line_-_Karachi_Metrobus "Green Line - Karachi Metrobus"), operational since December 2021, is part of a larger metrobus project financed by the Government of Pakistan and initiated in February 2016.[[471]](#cite_note-481)[[472]](#cite_note-482)[[473]](#cite_note-483) Meanwhile, [Faisalabad](/wiki/Faisalabad "Faisalabad") awaits its proposed rapid transit project, the [Faisalabad Metrobus](/wiki/Faisalabad_Metrobus "Faisalabad Metrobus").[[474]](#cite_note-484) [Karachi Circular Railway](/wiki/Karachi_Circular_Railway "Karachi Circular Railway"), partially revived in November 2020, offers public transit services in the Karachi metropolitan area.[[475]](#cite_note-485)[[476]](#cite_note-486) Additionally, plans are underway to resurrect Karachi's [tramway service](/wiki/Mohamedali_Tramways_Company "Mohamedali Tramways Company"), which ceased operations in 1975, in collaboration with Austrian experts.[[477]](#cite_note-487)[[478]](#cite_note-488)

As of 2013, Pakistan boasts approximately 151 airports and airfields, encompassing both military and [civilian](/wiki/Pakistan_Civil_Aviation_Authority "Pakistan Civil Aviation Authority") installations.[[479]](#cite_note-489) Despite [Jinnah International Airport](/wiki/Jinnah_International_Airport "Jinnah International Airport") serving as the primary international gateway, significant international traffic also flows through [Lahore](/wiki/Allama_Iqbal_International_Airport "Allama Iqbal International Airport"), [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad_International_Airport "Islamabad International Airport"), [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar_International_Airport "Peshawar International Airport"), [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta_International_Airport "Quetta International Airport"), [Faisalabad](/wiki/Faisalabad_International_Airport "Faisalabad International Airport"), [Sialkot](/wiki/Sialkot_International_Airport "Sialkot International Airport"), and [Multan](/wiki/Multan_International_Airport "Multan International Airport") airports. The civil aviation industry, deregulated in 1993, operates with a blend of [public](/wiki/Nationalisation_in_Pakistan "Nationalisation in Pakistan") and [private](/wiki/Privatisation_in_Pakistan "Privatisation in Pakistan") entities while [state-owned](/wiki/State_owned_enterprises "State owned enterprises") [Pakistan International Airlines](/wiki/Pakistan_International_Airlines "Pakistan International Airlines") (PIA) dominates, carrying 73% of domestic passengers and all domestic freight.

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in Pakistan](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Pakistan "Science and technology in Pakistan") and [List of Pakistani inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Pakistani_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Pakistani inventions and discoveries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg/250px-Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg)

[Abdus Salam](/wiki/Abdus_Salam "Abdus Salam") won the 1979 [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") for his contribution to [electroweak interaction](/wiki/Electroweak_interaction "Electroweak interaction"). He was the first Muslim to win a Nobel prize in science.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Atta-Ur-Rahman_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Atta-Ur-Rahman_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atta-Ur-Rahman_(cropped).jpg)

[Atta-ur-Rahman](/wiki/Atta_ur_Rahman_(scientist) "Atta ur Rahman (scientist)") won the [UNESCO Science Prize](/wiki/UNESCO_Science_Prize "UNESCO Science Prize") for pioneering contributions in chemistry in 1999, the first Muslim to win it.

Developments in [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Pakistan "Science and technology in Pakistan") have played a significant role in Pakistan's infrastructure, linking the nation to the global community.[[480]](#cite_note-490) Each year, the [Pakistan Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Pakistan_Academy_of_Sciences "Pakistan Academy of Sciences") and the government invite scientists worldwide to the [International Nathiagali Summer College on Physics](/wiki/International_Nathiagali_Summer_College_on_Physics "International Nathiagali Summer College on Physics").[[481]](#cite_note-491) In 2005, Pakistan hosted an international seminar on "Physics in Developing Countries" for the International Year of Physics.[[482]](#cite_note-492) Pakistani theoretical physicist [Abdus Salam](/wiki/Abdus_Salam "Abdus Salam") won a [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") for his work on the [electroweak interaction](/wiki/Electroweak_interaction "Electroweak interaction").[[483]](#cite_note-493) Pakistani scientists have made notable contributions in mathematics, biology, economics, [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), and [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics").[[484]](#cite_note-494)

In [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [Salimuzzaman Siddiqui](/wiki/Salimuzzaman_Siddiqui "Salimuzzaman Siddiqui") identified the medicinal properties of the [neem](/wiki/Neem "Neem") tree's components.[[485]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuniapanShaikh2007-495)[[486]](#cite_note-496) [Ayub K. Ommaya](/wiki/Ayub_K._Ommaya "Ayub K. Ommaya") developed the [Ommaya reservoir](/wiki/Ommaya_reservoir "Ommaya reservoir") for treating brain conditions.[[487]](#cite_note-497) Scientific research is integral to [Pakistani universities](/wiki/Pakistani_universities "Pakistani universities"), national laboratories, [science parks](/wiki/Science_park "Science park"), and the [industry](/wiki/Industry_of_Pakistan "Industry of Pakistan").[[488]](#cite_note-498) [Abdul Qadeer Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Qadeer_Khan "Abdul Qadeer Khan") spearheaded Pakistan's [HEU](/wiki/Enriched_uranium "Enriched uranium")-based [gas-centrifuge](/wiki/Zippe-type_centrifuge "Zippe-type centrifuge") [uranium enrichment](/wiki/Uranium_enrichment "Uranium enrichment") program for its [atomic bomb project](/wiki/Pakistan_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Pakistan and weapons of mass destruction").[[489]](#cite_note-499) He established the [Kahuta Research Laboratories](/wiki/Kahuta_Research_Laboratories "Kahuta Research Laboratories") (KRL) in 1976, serving as both its senior scientist and the Director-General until his retirement in 2001. Besides atomic bomb project, he made significant contributions in [molecular morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)"), physical [martensite](/wiki/Martensite "Martensite"), and their applications in [condensed](/wiki/Condensed_matter_physics "Condensed matter physics") and [material physics](/wiki/Material_physics "Material physics").[[490]](#cite_note-500)

In 2023, Pakistan ranked 26th globally in published scientific papers.[[491]](#cite_note-501) The influential [Pakistan Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Pakistan_Academy_of_Sciences "Pakistan Academy of Sciences") guides the government on science policies.[[492]](#cite_note-502) Pakistan was ranked 99th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") by 2025.[[493]](#cite_note-503)[[494]](#cite_note-504)

The 1960s marked the rise of Pakistan's space program, led by [SUPARCO](/wiki/Space_and_Upper_Atmosphere_Research_Commission "Space and Upper Atmosphere Research Commission"), yielding advancements in [rocketry](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics_Engineering "Electronics Engineering"), and [aeronomy](/wiki/Aeronomy "Aeronomy"). Notably, Pakistan launched its [first rocket](/wiki/Rehbar-I "Rehbar-I") into space, pioneering South Asia's space exploration.[[495]](#cite_note-505) In 1990, it successfully launched its [first satellite](/wiki/Badr-1 "Badr-1"), becoming the first Muslim nation and second in South Asia to achieve this milestone.[[496]](#cite_note-506)

> Pakistan witnessed a fourfold increase in its scientific productivity in the past decade surging from approximately 2,000 articles per year in 2006 to more than 9,000 articles in 2015. Making Pakistan's cited article's higher than the [BRIC](/wiki/BRIC_(economics_term) "BRIC (economics term)") countries put together.

—[Thomson Reuters](/wiki/Thomson_Reuters "Thomson Reuters")'s Another BRIC in the Wall 2016 report[[497]](#cite_note-507)

Following the [1971 war with India](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971"), Pakistan hastily developed [atomic weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") to deter foreign intervention and entered the [atomic age](/wiki/Atomic_age "Atomic age").[[498]](#cite_note-United_Book_Press.-508) Tensions with India led to Pakistan's [1998 underground](/wiki/List_of_nuclear_weapons_tests_of_Pakistan "List of nuclear weapons tests of Pakistan") [nuclear tests](/wiki/Chagai-I "Chagai-I"), making it the [seventh country](/wiki/Nuclear_club "Nuclear club") to possess such weapons.[[499]](#cite_note-509)

Pakistan is the sole Muslim nation active in Antarctica research, maintaining its [Jinnah Antarctic Research Station](/wiki/Pakistan_Antarctic_Programme "Pakistan Antarctic Programme") since 1992.[[500]](#cite_note-510) The government invests heavily in [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology_in_Pakistan "Information technology in Pakistan") projects, focusing on e-government and infrastructure.[[501]](#cite_note-511)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Pakistan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Pakistan "Demographics of Pakistan")

Further information: [Overseas Pakistani](/wiki/Overseas_Pakistani "Overseas Pakistani")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Population_Density_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg/250px-Population_Density_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_Density_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg)

Population Density per square kilometre of each Pakistani District as of the 2017 Pakistan Census

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Population_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg/250px-Population_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg)

Population of each Pakistani District as of the 2017 Pakistan Census

This section is an excerpt from [Demographics of Pakistan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Pakistan "Demographics of Pakistan").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Pakistan&action=edit)]

Pakistan had a population of 241,499,431 according to the final results of the [2023 census](/wiki/2023_Pakistani_census "2023 Pakistani census").[[502]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_2023Census-512) This figure includes the country's [four provinces](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") e.g. Punjab, Sindh, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Balochistan and the [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"). Azad Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan's census data is yet to be approved by CCI Council of Pakistan. Pakistan is the world's [fifth–most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population").[[503]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_UNWPP2019-513)

Between 1951 and 2023, Pakistan's population expanded over sevenfold, going from 33.7 million to 241.5 million. The country has a relatively high, although declining, growth rate supported by high birth rates and low death rates. Between 1998 and 2017, the average annual population growth rate stood at +2.40%.[[504]](#cite_note-514)

Dramatic social changes have led to urbanization and the emergence of two [megacities](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity"): [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") and [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"). The country's urban population more than tripled between 1981 and 2017 (from 23.8 million to 75.7 million), as Pakistan's [urbanisation rate](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") rose from 28.2% to 36.4%. Even with this, the nation's urbanisation rate remains one of [the lowest in the world](/wiki/Urbanization_by_sovereign_state "Urbanization by sovereign state"), and in 2017, over 130 million Pakistanis (making up nearly 65% of the population) lived in rural areas.

Due to a high [fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate"), which was estimated at 3.5 in 2022, Pakistan has one of the world's [youngest populations](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age"). The 2017 census recorded that 40.3% of the country's population was under the age of 15, while only 3.7% of Pakistanis were aged 65 or more.[[505]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_2017CensusAge-515) The [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of the country was 19,[[505]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_2017CensusAge-515) while its [sex ratio](/wiki/Sex_ratio "Sex ratio") was recorded to be 105 males per 100 females.[[506]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_2017CensusPopulation-516) Pakistan ranked last (148th of 148 countries) on the [Global Gender Gap Index](/wiki/Global_Gender_Gap_Report "Global Gender Gap Report"), with a gender parity score of 56.7%, reflecting one of the lowest levels of gender equality globally.[[507]](#cite_note-517)

The [demographic history of Pakistan](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Pakistan "Demographic history of Pakistan") from the ancient [Indus Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") to the modern era includes the arrival and settlement of many cultures and ethnic groups in the modern region of Pakistan from [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") and the nearby [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"). Because of this, Pakistan has a multicultural, multilinguistic, and multiethnic society. Pakistan is also thought to have the world's fourth-largest [refugee population](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_refugee_population "List of sovereign states by refugee population"), estimated at 1.4 million in mid-2021 by the [UNHCR](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees").[[508]](#cite_note-Demographics_of_Pakistan_RefugeesUNHCR-518)

### Urbanisation

Main article: [Urbanisation in Pakistan](/wiki/Urbanisation_in_Pakistan "Urbanisation in Pakistan")

Since [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Pakistan "Independence of Pakistan") due to the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation_in_Pakistan "Urbanisation in Pakistan") has surged for various reasons. In the south, [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") stands as the most populous commercial hub along the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River").[[509]](#cite_note-The_Urban_Frontier—Karachi-519) In the east, west, and north, a dense population arc spans cities like [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Faisalabad](/wiki/Faisalabad "Faisalabad"), [Rawalpindi](/wiki/Rawalpindi "Rawalpindi"), [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), [Sargodha](/wiki/Sargodha "Sargodha"), [Gujranwala](/wiki/Gujranwala "Gujranwala"), [Sialkot](/wiki/Sialkot "Sialkot"), [Gujrat](/wiki/Gujrat_city "Gujrat city"), [Jhelum](/wiki/Jhelum "Jhelum"), [Sheikhupura](/wiki/Sheikhupura "Sheikhupura"), [Nowshera](/wiki/Nowshera,_Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa "Nowshera, Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan "Mardan"), and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). By 1990–2008, city dwellers constituted 36% of Pakistan's population, making it South Asia's most urbanized nation, with over 50% living in towns of 5,000+ inhabitants.[[510]](#cite_note-Jason_Burke-520) [Immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Pakistan "Immigration to Pakistan"), both domestic and international, significantly fuels urban growth. Migration from India, especially to Karachi, the largest metropolis, and from nearby countries, accelerates urbanization, posing new political and socio-economic challenges. Economic shifts like the green revolution and political developments also play crucial roles.[[511]](#cite_note-Clark-521)
