### Ethnicity and languages

Main articles: [Languages of Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan") and [Ethnic groups of Pakistan](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_of_Pakistan "Ethnic groups of Pakistan")

See also: [Pakistanis](/wiki/Pakistanis "Pakistanis")

Pakistan is a diverse society with estimates suggesting it has between 75 and 85 languages.[[513]](#cite_note-523)[[514]](#cite_note-GlottologPK-524) Urdu and English serve as the official languages, with Urdu being the country's [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") and a unifying force among over 75% of Pakistanis.[[515]](#cite_note-525)[[516]](#cite_note-2017_Census-526) According to the [2023 national census](/wiki/2023_Pakistani_census "2023 Pakistani census"), the largest [ethnolinguistic groups](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") include the [Punjabis](/wiki/Punjabis "Punjabis") (36.98%), [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") (18.15%), [Sindhis](/wiki/Sindhis "Sindhis") (14.31%), [Saraikis](/wiki/Saraiki_people "Saraiki people") (12%), [Urdu speaking people](/wiki/Urdu_speaking_people "Urdu speaking people") (9.25%), [Balochs](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") (3.38%), [Hindkowans](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans")/[Hazarewals](/wiki/Hazarewal "Hazarewal") (2.32%), and [Brahuis](/wiki/Brahuis "Brahuis") (1.16%).[[517]](#cite_note-527)[[518]](#cite_note-auto-528) The remaining population consists of various ethnic minorities such as [Kashmiris](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris"), [Paharis](/wiki/Pahari_people_(Kashmir) "Pahari people (Kashmir)"), [Chitralis](/wiki/Chitralis "Chitralis"), various peoples of [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan"), [Kohistanis](/wiki/Indus_Kohistani_people "Indus Kohistani people"), [Torwalis](/wiki/Torwali_people "Torwali people"), [Meos](/wiki/Meo_(ethnic_group) "Meo (ethnic group)"), [Hazaras](/wiki/Hazaras "Hazaras"), [Kalash](/wiki/Kalash_people "Kalash people") and [Siddis](/wiki/Siddi "Siddi").[[519]](#cite_note-EB-Online-529)[[520]](#cite_note-530) The Pakistani diaspora, numbering over seven million, is the sixth largest in the world.[[521]](#cite_note-ribune-2016-531)

### Immigration

Main article: [Immigration to Pakistan](/wiki/Immigration_to_Pakistan "Immigration to Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Refugees_from_Afghanistan_in_Pakistan%2C_near_Islamabad.jpg/250px-Refugees_from_Afghanistan_in_Pakistan%2C_near_Islamabad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Refugees_from_Afghanistan_in_Pakistan,_near_Islamabad.jpg)

Afghan children near [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad") fetching water from water pump. (Pakistan once held the second largest refugee population globally after [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").[[522]](#cite_note-532))

Even post-1947 partition, the Muslims from India kept migrating to Pakistan, especially Karachi and Sindh province.[[523]](#cite_note-533) Wars in neighboring Afghanistan in the 1980s and 1990s pushed millions of [Afghan refugees](/wiki/Afghan_refugees "Afghan refugees") into Pakistan, mainly in Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa and [tribal areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas"), with some in Karachi and Quetta. Pakistan hosts one of the world's largest refugee populations.[[524]](#cite_note-534) Additionally, around 2 million [Bengalis](/wiki/Bengalis_in_Pakistan "Bengalis in Pakistan") and half a million undocumented individuals, purportedly from [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), reside in Pakistan.[[525]](#cite_note-535) Pakistan ordered the [mass expulsion of undocumented Afghans](/wiki/Deportation_of_undocumented_Afghans_from_Pakistan "Deportation of undocumented Afghans from Pakistan") in October 2023, citing national security risks.[[526]](#cite_note-536)

Migration of Bengalis and [Rohingya](/wiki/Rohingya_people "Rohingya people") to Pakistan started in the 1980s and continued till 1998. Karachi hosts a significant number of Bengali settlements, and large Rohingya migration made it one of their largest populations outside Myanmar.[[527]](#cite_note-537) Karachi's [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_people "Burmese people") community resides in various slums across the city.[[528]](#cite_note-538)

According to [BBC](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service"), thousands of [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") Muslims live in Gilgit-Baltistan, some left Xinjiang, China and the thriving trading town of Kashgar in 1949, while others are later arrivals, claiming to escape political oppression.[[529]](#cite_note-539) Since 1989, thousands of [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris") Muslim refugees fled to Pakistan, alleging rape and forced displacement by Indian soldiers.[[530]](#cite_note-540)

### Diaspora

Main article: [Overseas Pakistani](/wiki/Overseas_Pakistani "Overseas Pakistani")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Nergis_Mavalvala.jpg/250px-Nergis_Mavalvala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nergis_Mavalvala.jpg)

[Nergis Mavalvala](/wiki/Nergis_Mavalvala "Nergis Mavalvala") is a [Pakistani American](/wiki/Pakistani_Americans "Pakistani Americans") Professor of Physics at [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") who is known for her role in the first observation of gravitational waves.

According to the UN Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Pakistan has the sixth-largest diaspora globally.[[521]](#cite_note-ribune-2016-531) Approximately 7 million Pakistanis reside abroad, mainly in the Middle East, Europe, and North America.[[531]](#cite_note-541) Pakistan ranks 10th globally for remittances sent home.[[532]](#cite_note-overseaspakistanis1-542)[[533]](#cite_note-worldbank1-543) Saudi Arabia is the largest source of remittances, contributing $5.9 billion as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pakistan&action=edit).[[534]](#cite_note-544) The term *[Overseas Pakistani](/wiki/Overseas_Pakistani "Overseas Pakistani")* is officially recognized by the Government of Pakistan, with the [Ministry of Overseas Pakistanis and Human Resource Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Overseas_Pakistanis "Ministry of Overseas Pakistanis") addressing their needs, welfare, and issues. Overseas Pakistanis constitute the second-largest source of foreign exchange remittances to Pakistan, with remittances increasing by over 100% from US$8.9 billion in 2009–10 to US$19.9 billion in 2015–16.[[535]](#cite_note-remit-545)[[533]](#cite_note-worldbank1-543)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Pakistan](/wiki/Religion_in_Pakistan "Religion in Pakistan")

Religions in Pakistan (2023 census)[[536]](#cite_note-2023_Census-546)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Religions | |  | Percent | |
| [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Pakistan "Islam in Pakistan") | |  | 96.3% | |
| [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan") | |  | 2.2% | |
| [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Pakistan "Christianity in Pakistan") | |  | 1.4% | |
| [others](/wiki/Religion_in_Pakistan#Demographics_of_religion_in_Pakistan "Religion in Pakistan") | |  | 0.1% | |

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Pakistan "Islam in Pakistan") is the state religion,[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMunir1975-228) with freedom of religion guaranteed by the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan").[[537]](#cite_note-CoP-547)[[538]](#cite_note-548) The majority are Muslims (96.35%), followed by [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan") (2.17%) and [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Pakistan "Christianity in Pakistan") (1.37%). Minorities include [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Pakistan "Sikhism in Pakistan"), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Pakistan "Buddhism in Pakistan"), [Jains](/wiki/Jainism_in_Pakistan "Jainism in Pakistan"), [Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") ([Parsi](/wiki/Parsis "Parsis")), and the unique [Kalash people](/wiki/Kalash_people "Kalash people") who practice [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism").[[539]](#cite_note-549) In 2012, 2% of the population identified as atheist in a [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_Pakistan "Gallup Pakistan") survey.[[540]](#cite_note-550)

#### Islam

Main article: [Islam in Pakistan](/wiki/Islam_in_Pakistan "Islam in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Faisal_Masjid.jpg/250px-Faisal_Masjid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faisal_Masjid.jpg)

[Faisal Mosque](/wiki/Faisal_Mosque "Faisal Mosque"), built in 1986 by Turkish architect [Vedat Dalokay](/wiki/Vedat_Dalokay "Vedat Dalokay") on behalf of [King](/wiki/House_of_Saud "House of Saud") [Faisal bin Abdul-Aziz](/wiki/Faisal_bin_Abdul-Aziz "Faisal bin Abdul-Aziz") of Saudi Arabia

Islam dominates in Pakistan, with about 96.35% of the population being Muslim.[[541]](#cite_note-551) Pakistan ranks second globally in Muslim population,[[542]](#cite_note-552) and is home to 10.5% of the world's Muslims.[[543]](#cite_note-553) Karachi is the largest Muslim city in the world.[[544]](#cite_note-554)

The majority follow [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), with a significant presence of [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism_in_Pakistan "Sufism in Pakistan"), while Shia Muslims constitute a minority.[[545]](#cite_note-LoC2-555)[[546]](#cite_note-556) Shias represent between 5–25%.[[545]](#cite_note-LoC2-555)[[547]](#cite_note-ciafactbook-557)[[548]](#cite_note-558) The Shia population in Pakistan was estimated at 42 million in 2019.[[549]](#cite_note-559) As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pakistan&action=edit), 12% of Pakistani Muslims self-identify as [non-denominational Muslims](/wiki/Non-denominational_Muslims "Non-denominational Muslims").[[550]](#cite_note-560)

The [Ahmadis](/wiki/Ahmadiyya_in_Pakistan "Ahmadiyya in Pakistan") are a minority, officially considered non-Muslims.[[551]](#cite_note-561)[[552]](#cite_note-562) Ahmadis face persecution, banned from calling themselves Muslims since 1974.[[553]](#cite_note-563)

#### Hinduism

Main article: [Hinduism in Pakistan](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Hindu_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg/500px-Hindu_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hindu_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg)

Hindu proportion of each Pakistani District in 2017 according to the Pakistan Bureau of Statistics

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan") is the second-largest religion, followed by 2.17% of the population according to the census in 2023.[[536]](#cite_note-2023_Census-546) Pakistan had the fifth-largest Hindu population globally in 2010.[[554]](#cite_note-564) In 2023, Hindus numbered 5,217,216.[[536]](#cite_note-2023_Census-546)[[k]](#cite_note-565) They reside across Pakistan but are concentrated in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), where they make up 8.81% of the population.[[536]](#cite_note-2023_Census-546) [Umerkot district](/wiki/Umerkot_district "Umerkot district") of the province is the only Hindu majority area. [Tharparkar district](/wiki/Tharparkar_district "Tharparkar district") hosts the largest Hindu population. Four districts – Umerkot, Tharparkar, [Mirpurkhas](/wiki/Mirpurkhas_District "Mirpurkhas District"), and [Sanghar](/wiki/Sanghar_District "Sanghar District") – have over half of Pakistan's Hindus.[[555]](#cite_note-566)

At Pakistan's inception, the 'hostage theory' suggested fair treatment of Hindus to safeguard Muslims in India.[[556]](#cite_note-567)[[557]](#cite_note-Dhulipala-2015-6-568) However, some Pakistani Hindus felt marginalized, leading to emigration to India.[[558]](#cite_note-bbc200703022-569) They faced violence post the [Babri Masjid demolition](/wiki/Babri_Masjid_demolition "Babri Masjid demolition").[[559]](#cite_note-570)

#### Christianity and other religions

Main article: [Christianity in Pakistan](/wiki/Christianity_in_Pakistan "Christianity in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Christian_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg/500px-Christian_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Christian_Proportion_by_Pakistani_District_-_2017_Census.svg)

Christian proportion of each Pakistani District in 2017 according to the Pakistan Bureau of Statistics

Christians are the next largest religious minority after Hindus, constituting 1.37% of the population.[[536]](#cite_note-2023_Census-546) They are concentrated in [Lahore District](/wiki/Lahore_District "Lahore District") (5%) and [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory") (over 4%). Karachi hosts a historic [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Pakistan "Roman Catholicism in Pakistan") community established by [Goan](/wiki/Goa "Goa") and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people") migrants during British colonial rule.[[560]](#cite_note-Districtwise-571)

Following Christianity, the [Bahá'í Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_Faith_in_Pakistan "Bahá'í Faith in Pakistan") had 30,000 followers in 2008, followed by Sikhism, Buddhism, and [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), each with around 20,000 adherents in 2008,[[561]](#cite_note-572) alongside a small [Jain community](/wiki/Jainism_in_Pakistan "Jainism in Pakistan").

### Education

Main article: [Education in Pakistan](/wiki/Education_in_Pakistan "Education in Pakistan")

See also: [Higher Education Commission (Pakistan)](/wiki/Higher_Education_Commission_(Pakistan) "Higher Education Commission (Pakistan)") and [Rankings of universities in Pakistan](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_Pakistan "Rankings of universities in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/NUST_MainOffice.png/250px-NUST_MainOffice.png)](/wiki/File:NUST_MainOffice.png)

[NUST](/wiki/NUST_School_of_Electrical_Engineering_and_Computer_Science "NUST School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science") in Islamabad is a top ranked Engineering University.

Pakistan's [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan") mandates [free](/wiki/Free_education "Free education") primary and secondary education,[[562]](#cite_note-573) with public universities established in each province, including [Punjab University](/wiki/University_of_the_Punjab "University of the Punjab"), [Sindh University](/wiki/Sindh_University "Sindh University"), [Peshawar University](/wiki/University_of_Peshawar "University of Peshawar"), [Karachi University](/wiki/University_of_Karachi "University of Karachi"), and [Balochistan University](/wiki/University_of_Balochistan "University of Balochistan"). The country's educational landscape encompasses both [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") and [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") universities, fostering collaboration to enhance research and [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Pakistan "Higher education in Pakistan") opportunities, albeit with concerns regarding teaching quality in newer institutions.[[563]](#cite_note-574) [Technical and vocational institutions](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") in Pakistan number approximately 3,193,[[564]](#cite_note-edu2-575) complemented by *[madrassahs](/wiki/Madrassas_in_Pakistan "Madrassas in Pakistan")* providing free Islamic education to students,[[565]](#cite_note-576) with government efforts to [regulate and monitor](/wiki/Education_reform "Education reform") their quality amidst concerns over extremists recruitment.[[566]](#cite_note-577) [Education](/wiki/Education_in_Pakistan "Education in Pakistan") is divided into six main levels, including nursery, primary, [middle](/wiki/Middle_school "Middle school"), [matriculation](/wiki/High_school_(upper_secondary) "High school (upper secondary)"), [intermediate](/wiki/Community_college "Community college"), and university programs.[[564]](#cite_note-edu2-575) Additionally, [private schools](/wiki/Schools_in_Pakistan "Schools in Pakistan") offer a parallel secondary education system based on the curriculum set by the [Cambridge International Examinations](/wiki/Cambridge_International_Examinations "Cambridge International Examinations"),[[567]](#cite_note-578) with 439 international schools reported in Pakistan.[[568]](#cite_note-579)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Malala_Yousafzai_and_Kaliash_Satyarthi_at_the_Nobel_Peace_Prize_ceremony.jpg/250px-Malala_Yousafzai_and_Kaliash_Satyarthi_at_the_Nobel_Peace_Prize_ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malala_Yousafzai_and_Kaliash_Satyarthi_at_the_Nobel_Peace_Prize_ceremony.jpg)

[Malala Yousafzai](/wiki/Malala_Yousafzai "Malala Yousafzai") was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 2014, alongside [Kailash Satyarthi](/wiki/Kailash_Satyarthi "Kailash Satyarthi") of India, for her advocacy of educational initiatives, particularly girls' education worldwide.

Initiatives since 2007 made [English medium education](/wiki/English_medium_education "English medium education") mandatory nationwide. Following a 2012 attack on activist [Malala Yousafzai](/wiki/Malala_Yousafzai "Malala Yousafzai") by the [Taliban](/wiki/Tehrik-i_Taliban_Pakistan "Tehrik-i Taliban Pakistan"), she became the youngest Nobel laureate for her education advocacy.[[569]](#cite_note-580) Reforms in 2013 mandated Chinese language courses in Sindh, reflecting China's growing influence. As of 2018, Pakistan's literacy rate stands at 62.3%, with significant regional and gender disparities.[[570]](#cite_note-581) Government initiatives, including computer literacy since 1995, aim to eradicate illiteracy, targeting 100% enrollment among primary school-age children and an ~86% literacy rate by 2015.[[571]](#cite_note-582) Pakistan allocates 2.3% of its GDP to education,[[572]](#cite_note-583) among the lowest in South Asia.[[573]](#cite_note-584)

## Culture

Main articles: [Culture of Pakistan](/wiki/Culture_of_Pakistan "Culture of Pakistan"), [British heritage of Pakistan](/wiki/British_heritage_of_Pakistan "British heritage of Pakistan"), and [Public holidays in Pakistan](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Pakistan "Public holidays in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Ceiling_of_Frere_Hall.jpg/250px-Ceiling_of_Frere_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceiling_of_Frere_Hall.jpg)

Artwork by [Sadequain](/wiki/Sadequain "Sadequain") on the ceiling of [Frere Hall](/wiki/Frere_Hall "Frere Hall"). Having painted around 15,000 paintings, Sadequain is considered one of the finest painters and calligraphers Pakistan has ever produced.

[Civil society](/wiki/Culture_of_Pakistan "Culture of Pakistan") in Pakistan is hierarchical, emphasizing [local cultural etiquette](/wiki/Etiquette_in_Pakistan "Etiquette in Pakistan") and traditional Islamic values. The primary family unit is the [extended family](/wiki/Extended_family "Extended family"), but there's a rising trend towards [nuclear families](/wiki/Nuclear_families "Nuclear families") due to socio-economic factors.[[574]](#cite_note-nuclear-585)[[575]](#cite_note-586) Both men and women typically wear *[Shalwar Kameez](/wiki/Shalwar_Kameez "Shalwar Kameez")*; men also favor trousers, jeans, and shirts.[[576]](#cite_note-taxila-587) The middle class has grown to about 35 million, with another 17 million in the upper and upper-middle classes, leading to a shift in power from rural landowners to urban elites.[[577]](#cite_note-588) Festivals like *[Eid ul-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_ul-Fitr "Eid ul-Fitr")*, *[Eid ul-Azha](/wiki/Eid_ul-Azha "Eid ul-Azha")*, [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"), [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), [Holi](/wiki/Holi "Holi"), and [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali") are primarily religious.[[574]](#cite_note-nuclear-585) Pakistan ranked 56th on the 2006 [A.T. Kearney](/wiki/A.T._Kearney "A.T. Kearney")/FP [Globalization Index](/wiki/Globalization_Index "Globalization Index") due to increasing globalization.[[578]](#cite_note-589)

### Architecture

Main articles: [Pakistani architecture](/wiki/Pakistani_architecture "Pakistani architecture") and [Hindu, Jain and Buddhist architectural heritage of Pakistan](/wiki/Hindu,_Jain_and_Buddhist_architectural_heritage_of_Pakistan "Hindu, Jain and Buddhist architectural heritage of Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Lahore_Fort.jpg/250px-Lahore_Fort.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lahore_Fort.jpg)

The [Lahore Fort](/wiki/Lahore_Fort "Lahore Fort"), a landmark built during the Mughal era, is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")

Four periods define Pakistani architecture: [pre-Islamic](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan#Early_history "History of Pakistan"), [Islamic](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan#Muslim_period "History of Pakistan"), [colonial](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan#Colonial_era "History of Pakistan"), and [post-colonial](/wiki/History_of_Pakistan#Independence "History of Pakistan"). The onset of the [Indus civilization](/wiki/Indus_civilization "Indus civilization") around the mid-3rd millennium BCE heralded an urban culture, evidenced by surviving large structures.[[579]](#cite_note-590) Notable pre-Islamic settlements include [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro"), [Harappa](/wiki/Harappa "Harappa"), and [Kot Diji](/wiki/Kot_Diji "Kot Diji").[[580]](#cite_note-mountains-591) The fusion of Buddhism and [Greek](/wiki/Indian_campaign_of_Alexander_the_Great "Indian campaign of Alexander the Great") influences birthed a distinctive [Greco-Buddhist](/wiki/Greco-Buddhism "Greco-Buddhism") style from the 1st century CE, exemplified by the renowned [Gandhara style](/wiki/Gandara_art "Gandara art").[[581]](#cite_note-592) Notable [Buddhist architectural](/wiki/Buddhist_architecture "Buddhist architecture") remnants include the [Takht-i-Bahi](/wiki/Takht-i-Bahi "Takht-i-Bahi") monastery in [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa").[[582]](#cite_note-593)

The advent of Islam in present-day Pakistan marked the cessation of Buddhist architecture, ushering in [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture "Islamic architecture"). The notable [Indo-Islamic](/wiki/Indo-Islamic_architecture "Indo-Islamic architecture") structure, the [tomb of Shah Rukn-i-Alam](/wiki/Rukn-e-Alam "Rukn-e-Alam") in Multan, remains significant. During the Mughal era, Persian-Islamic design merged with Hindustani art, seen in Lahore's architectural gems like the [Badshahi Mosque](/wiki/Badshahi_Mosque "Badshahi Mosque") and the [Lahore Fort](/wiki/Lahore_Fort "Lahore Fort") with the iconic [Alamgiri Gate](/wiki/Alamgiri_Gate "Alamgiri Gate"). Lahore also boasts the vibrant [Wazir Khan Mosque](/wiki/Wazir_Khan_Mosque "Wazir Khan Mosque"),[[583]](#cite_note-594) and the lush [Shalimar Gardens](/wiki/Shalimar_Gardens_(Lahore) "Shalimar Gardens (Lahore)"). In the British colonial period, Indo-European buildings emerged, blending European and Indian-Islamic styles. Post-colonial identity shines through modern landmarks like the [Faisal Mosque](/wiki/Faisal_Mosque "Faisal Mosque"), [Minar-e-Pakistan](/wiki/Minar-e-Pakistan "Minar-e-Pakistan"), and [Mazar-e-Quaid](/wiki/Mazar-e-Quaid "Mazar-e-Quaid"). [British architectural](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Architecture of the United Kingdom") influence persists in structures across Lahore, Peshawar, and Karachi.[[584]](#cite_note-595)

### Clothing, arts, and fashion

Main articles: [Pakistani clothing](/wiki/Pakistani_clothing "Pakistani clothing"), [Shalwar kameez](/wiki/Shalwar_kameez "Shalwar kameez"), [Sherwani](/wiki/Sherwani "Sherwani"), [Jinnah cap](/wiki/Jinnah_cap "Jinnah cap"), [Peshawari chappal](/wiki/Peshawari_chappal "Peshawari chappal"), [Pakol](/wiki/Pakol "Pakol"), and [Sindhi topi](/wiki/Sindhi_topi "Sindhi topi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Traditional_clothing_from_Sindh.jpg/250px-Traditional_clothing_from_Sindh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_clothing_from_Sindh.jpg)

A depiction of traditional clothing of women from [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh")

The *[Shalwar kameez](/wiki/Shalwar_kameez "Shalwar kameez")* is Pakistan's [national dress](/wiki/National_dress "National dress"), worn in all provinces: Punjab, Sindh, Balochistan, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and Azad Kashmir. Each province has its own style. Pakistanis wear a variety of fabrics like silk, [chiffon](/wiki/Chiffon_(fabric) "Chiffon (fabric)"), and cotton. In addition to the national dress, men often wear domestically tailored suits and [neckties](/wiki/Necktie "Necktie"), especially in offices, schools, and social gatherings.[[585]](#cite_note-596)

Pakistan's [fashion industry](/wiki/Pakistani_clothing "Pakistani clothing") has thrived, blending traditional and modern styles to create a unique cultural identity. Regional and traditional dress remain significant symbols of native tradition, evolving into both modern and purer forms. Organizations like the Pakistan Fashion Design Council in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") and the Fashion Pakistan Council in [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") host events like [PFDC Fashion Week](/wiki/PFDC_Sunsilk_Fashion_Week "PFDC Sunsilk Fashion Week") and [Fashion Pakistan Week](/wiki/Fashion_Pakistan_Week "Fashion Pakistan Week"). Pakistan's inaugural fashion week took place in November 2009.[[586]](#cite_note-597)

### Literature and philosophy

Main articles: [Literature of Pakistan](/wiki/Literature_of_Pakistan "Literature of Pakistan"), [Urdu poetry](/wiki/Urdu_poetry "Urdu poetry"), and [Pakistani philosophy](/wiki/Pakistani_philosophy "Pakistani philosophy")

[![Muhammad Iqbal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8a/Iqbal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iqbal.jpg)

[Muhammad Iqbal](/wiki/Muhammad_Iqbal "Muhammad Iqbal"), Pakistan's national poet who conceived the [idea](/wiki/Conception_of_Pakistan "Conception of Pakistan") of Pakistan

Pakistan boasts literature in various languages including [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_literature "Urdu literature"), [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_literature "Sindhi literature"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_literature "Punjabi literature"), [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_literature_and_poetry "Pashto literature and poetry"), [Baluchi](/wiki/Balochi_Academy "Balochi Academy"), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature"), [English](/wiki/Pakistani_English_literature "Pakistani English literature"), and more.[[587]](#cite_note-598) The [Pakistan Academy of Letters](/wiki/Pakistan_Academy_of_Letters "Pakistan Academy of Letters") actively promotes literature and poetry both domestically and internationally.[[588]](#cite_note-599) [National Library](/wiki/National_Library_of_Pakistan "National Library of Pakistan") contributes to literary dissemination. Historically, Pakistani literature consisted mainly of [lyric](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry"), [religious](/wiki/Sufi_poetry "Sufi poetry"), and [folkloric](/wiki/Pakistani_folklore "Pakistani folklore") works, later diversifying under colonial influence into prose fiction, now widely embraced.[[589]](#cite_note-600)[[590]](#cite_note-granta-601)

The [national poet](/wiki/List_of_national_poets "List of national poets") of Pakistan, [Muhammad Iqbal](/wiki/Muhammad_Iqbal "Muhammad Iqbal"), wrote influential poetry in Urdu and Persian, advocating for Islamic civilizational revival.[[591]](#cite_note-602) Notable figures in contemporary Urdu literature include [Josh Malihabadi](/wiki/Josh_Malihabadi "Josh Malihabadi"), [Faiz Ahmed Faiz](/wiki/Faiz_Ahmed_Faiz "Faiz Ahmed Faiz"), and [Saadat Hasan Manto](/wiki/Saadat_Hasan_Manto "Saadat Hasan Manto").[[590]](#cite_note-granta-601) Popular Sufi poets like [Shah Abdul Latif](/wiki/Shah_Abdul_Latif "Shah Abdul Latif") and [Bulleh Shah](/wiki/Bulleh_Shah "Bulleh Shah") are revered.[[592]](#cite_note-603) [Mirza Kalich Beg](/wiki/Mirza_Kalich_Beg "Mirza Kalich Beg") is hailed as the father of modern Sindhi prose.[[593]](#cite_note-604) Pakistani philosophy has been shaped by influences from British and American philosophy, with notable figures like [M. M. Sharif](/wiki/M._M._Sharif "M. M. Sharif") contributing to its development.[[594]](#cite_note-605) Post-1971, Marxist thought gained prominence in Pakistani philosophy through figures like [Jalaludin Abdur Rahim](/wiki/Jalaludin_Abdur_Rahim "Jalaludin Abdur Rahim").[[595]](#cite_note-606)

### Media and entertainment

Main articles: [Mass media in Pakistan](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Pakistan "Mass media in Pakistan"), [Cinema of Pakistan](/wiki/Cinema_of_Pakistan "Cinema of Pakistan"), [Music of Pakistan](/wiki/Music_of_Pakistan "Music of Pakistan"), [History of Pakistani pop music](/wiki/History_of_Pakistani_pop_music "History of Pakistani pop music"), [Theatre of Pakistan](/wiki/Theatre_of_Pakistan "Theatre of Pakistan"), and [Pakistani dramas](/wiki/Pakistani_dramas "Pakistani dramas")

The private [print media](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Pakistan "List of newspapers in Pakistan"), state-owned [Pakistan Television Corporation](/wiki/Pakistan_Television_Corporation "Pakistan Television Corporation") (PTV), and [Pakistan Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Pakistan_Broadcasting_Corporation "Pakistan Broadcasting Corporation") (PBC) dominated media until the 21st century. Pakistan now boasts a vast network of domestic, privately owned [24-hour electronic](/wiki/24-hour_news_cycle "24-hour news cycle") [news media](/wiki/List_of_television_channels_in_Pakistan "List of television channels in Pakistan") and [cable](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") [television channels](/wiki/Television_in_Pakistan "Television in Pakistan").[[596]](#cite_note-607) The [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders") has indicated pressure faced by Pakistani reporters, particularly when reporting against the military or government.[[597]](#cite_note-RSF-608) The BBC describes Pakistani media as "among the most outspoken in South Asia".[[598]](#cite_note-609) Pakistani media has been instrumental in exposing corruption.[[599]](#cite_note-610)

The [Lollywood](/wiki/Lollywood "Lollywood"), Punjabi, and [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_cinema "Pashto cinema") film industry is centered in Karachi, Lahore, and Peshawar. Although Bollywood films were banned from public cinemas from 1965 to 2008, they remained influential in Pakistani popular culture.[[600]](#cite_note-611) However, in 2019, the screening of Bollywood movies faced an indefinite ban.[[601]](#cite_note-612) Despite challenges faced by the Pakistani film industry, Urdu [televised dramas](/wiki/Pakistani_dramas "Pakistani dramas") and theatrical performances remain popular, frequently broadcast by many entertainment media outlets.[[602]](#cite_note-613) Urdu dramas dominate the television entertainment industry, renowned for their quality since the 1990s.[[603]](#cite_note-614) Pakistani music encompasses diverse forms, from provincial folk music and traditional styles like [Qawwali](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali") and [Ghazal](/wiki/Ghazal "Ghazal") Gayaki to modern fusions of traditional and western music.[[604]](#cite_note-615) Pakistan boasts numerous renowned folk singers, and the arrival of Afghan refugees in western provinces has sparked interest in Pashto music, despite occasional intolerance.[[605]](#cite_note-616)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Pakistani cuisine](/wiki/Pakistani_cuisine "Pakistani cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/2_Chapati_warm_and_ready_to_be_eaten.jpg/250px-2_Chapati_warm_and_ready_to_be_eaten.jpg)](/wiki/File:2_Chapati_warm_and_ready_to_be_eaten.jpg)

[Roti](/wiki/Roti "Roti"), served with various side dishes, is considered a staple food in Pakistan

Pakistani cuisine, rooted in the royal kitchens of 16th-century Mughal emperors, blends influences from [British](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine"), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine"), [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asian_cuisine "Central Asian cuisine"), and [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_cuisine "Middle Eastern cuisine") culinary traditions.[[606]](#cite_note-617) Unlike Middle Eastern fare, Pakistani dishes are heavily spiced with garlic, ginger, turmeric, [chili](/wiki/Chili_powder "Chili powder"), and [garam masala](/wiki/Garam_masala "Garam masala"). [Roti](/wiki/Roti "Roti"), a wheat-based flatbread, accompanies most meals, alongside [curry](/wiki/Curry "Curry"), meat, vegetables, and lentils. Rice is also common, served plain, spiced, or in sweet dishes.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMohiuddin20073,_317,_323–324-181)[[607]](#cite_note-618) [Lassi](/wiki/Lassi "Lassi"), a traditional drink from the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), and [black tea with milk and sugar](/wiki/Pakistani_tea_culture "Pakistani tea culture") are popular beverages enjoyed nationwide.[[576]](#cite_note-taxila-587)[[608]](#cite_note-619) [Sohan halwa](/wiki/Sohan_halwa "Sohan halwa"), a beloved sweet dish from southern Punjab, is savored across Pakistan.[[609]](#cite_note-620)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Pakistan](/wiki/Sport_in_Pakistan "Sport in Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Pakvaus.jpg/250px-Pakvaus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pakvaus.jpg)

A cricket match between Pakistan and Australia at [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Hockey_Nederland_tegen_Pakistan_3-1%2C_Amsterdam%2C_de_Wereldcup_die_Pakistan_onlan%E2%80%A6%2C_Bestanddeelnr_925-0964.jpg/250px-Hockey_Nederland_tegen_Pakistan_3-1%2C_Amsterdam%2C_de_Wereldcup_die_Pakistan_onlan%E2%80%A6%2C_Bestanddeelnr_925-0964.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hockey_Nederland_tegen_Pakistan_3-1,_Amsterdam,_de_Wereldcup_die_Pakistan_onlan%E2%80%A6,_Bestanddeelnr_925-0964.jpg)

The [Pakistan national hockey team](/wiki/Pakistan_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Pakistan men's national field hockey team") with the [1971 Hockey World Cup trophy](/wiki/1971_Men%27s_Hockey_World_Cup "1971 Men's Hockey World Cup")

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") is the most popular sport in Pakistan, followed by [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). [Field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") is the national sport. Other sports like [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo"), and [traditional games](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_Pakistan "Traditional games of Pakistan") are also enjoyed.

In cricket, Pakistan boasts victories in all major [ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") tournaments, including the [ICC Cricket World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Cricket_World_Cup "ICC Cricket World Cup"), [ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/ICC_World_Twenty20 "ICC World Twenty20"), and [ICC Champions Trophy](/wiki/ICC_Champions_Trophy "ICC Champions Trophy"). The [Pakistan Super League](/wiki/Pakistan_Super_League "Pakistan Super League") ranks among the top [T20 leagues](/wiki/List_of_T20_cricket_competitions "List of T20 cricket competitions") globally.[[610]](#cite_note-621)[[611]](#cite_note-622)

In football, Pakistan established the [Pakistan Football Federation](/wiki/Pakistan_Football_Federation "Pakistan Football Federation") soon after its creation, and it is known for producing [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") balls.[[612]](#cite_note-623)[[613]](#cite_note-624)

In field hockey, Pakistan boasts four [Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Hockey_World_Cup "Men's FIH Hockey World Cup") wins, eight [Asian Games](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Asian_Games "Field hockey at the Asian Games") gold medals, and three [Olympic](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") gold medals. Squash player [Jahangir Khan](/wiki/Jahangir_Khan "Jahangir Khan") holds the record for the longest winning streak in professional sport history, winning 555 consecutive matches.[[614]](#cite_note-625)[[615]](#cite_note-626) Pakistan has hosted various international events, including Cricket and Hockey World Cups and Asian Games.[[616]](#cite_note-ICC-2021-627)

Like a number of his contemporaries, Iqbal warmed up to the two nation-theory.
